VI. 1. 157; see also p. 157. परिणतपतङ्ग &c. —परिणत ripened, about to set; पतङ्ग the evening sun ; स एव बालपल्लवः सोऽवतंसः सञ्जातोऽस्य, wearing the head ornament of tender sprouts in the form of the evening sun. उपास्य सन्ध्यां— having performed the evening twilight rites. समीकृत—were levelled (being covered up by darkness). निन्मोन्नत—the high and the low places, irregularities. शरारु—शृणाति इति (fr. शृ+आरु); ferocious animals. शर्व—Śiva. शार्वर—nocturnal, nightly. आध्यात—covered, screoned. "In which the deep caverns were filled up (covered) with the mass (flood) of nocturnal darkness, dark like" &c. Mark the recurrence the letter श in these three lines.

** P. 138**. एकक—एक एव एकाकी or एककः (aff कन्); Pân V. 3. 52. उपधाय—using it as a pillow. असुखायिषत किमपि—felt an indescribable pleasure. अभ्यमनायिष्ट—felt cheered or delighted. असुखायिषत and अभ्यमनायिष्ट are Aor, forms of सुखायते ( सुखं वेदयते) and अभिमनायते denom verbs fr. सुख (when the agent experiences the pleasure; see com.) and मनस् 'with अभि by adding क्यङ् (4) आह्नदयिषता Aor. 3rd pers pl of the I. A. to feel pleasure. हृषिज्ञाः—thrilled, bristled. p. p. p. of हृष् 4 P. to stand on end, as hair; the form is also हृष्ट, हृ being optionally prefixed to त in this sense by हृषेर्लोमसु Páṇ. VII. 2. 29. दृष्ट, हृषितं लोभ ; see Sid. -Kau. for further particulars. पर्यस्फ़ुरत &c. —the throbbing of the right arm in the case of males indicates some impending good fortune; cf. स्फ़ुरति च बाहुः कुतः फ़लमिहास्य¸Śàk. I. 15 and our notes ad loc; see also Ragh. VI. 68. वितान—a canopy. समया—near. चित्रास्तरणं—a bed of diverse colours; चित्रास्तरण् may also probably mean 'carpets, of various colours.' आगलित—a little displaced. अमृत &c. —अमृत water; ०फेनपटलवत्, पटलमेव च यद् &c. आदिवराह—The primeval Boar (Vishņu's 3rd incarnation) that lifted the Earth out of the waters in which she was sinking. See our vote on रसातलादादीभवेन पुंसा &c. Ragh. XIII. 8; and of. Vishņu, P ततः समुत्क्षिप्य धरां स्वदंष्ट्र्या महावराहः स्फुटपद्मलोचनः। रसातलादुत्पलपत्रसन्निभः समुत्थितो नील इवाचलो महान्॥ अंसस्रस्त &c. —This corresponds to आगलितस्तनांशुकां; as the earth emerged from the sea the oceanio waters rolled back from her head, and so she looked like the princess with her upper garment slipped off from her bosom

 भयसाध्वस—The meaning assigned to भय by the Com. is far-fetched and inappropriate, as it cannot be taken with the princess. The princess was in deep sleep and the earth was also motionless on account of the trance. The tremor was due to tho stroke of the snout of the Boar; of येन प्रलयवराहघोणाभिघातभीति भूतधात्री &c. Kád. Hence the two are compared here साध्वस—agilation, perturbation. लास्य—dancing, throbbing. As her breath passed along the lower lip, the rays issuing from it seemed to throb. The poet compares her lower lip, which excited passion, to a spark of the burnt body of Oupid, and she was blowing it up as it were into a flame. षट्पद—a bee. जातनिद्रं—(1) closed; (2) closed in sleep. लोचनेन्दीवर—The blue-lotus-like eye takes the place of the bee confined in the closed lotus. Cf. Bhatti. IV. 19; Ragh. V. 68. ऐरावत &o. —first plucked off and then cast away (lying disorderly) वल्लरी—a branch.

** P. 189.** ऊर्ध्वाण्डकपाल—the upper half of the universal egg, i . e. the vault of the sky. उदर—the convex cavity of. शक्तिध्वज—Kárttikeya. शिखर—the dome of his temple. शूल—the trident on its summit. ०उत्सेधं—lofty like. According to Laghudîpika (a short com, on the D. -K. ; see the quotation In the com. ) it means ‘a palace having the god Kárttikeya set on the ground floor and the trident on the dome or top, and technically called Nandyávarta.' This sense seems to be preferable to that given by the Bhúshaṇâ, viz. 'as lofty as the temple of Kârttikeya with the trident on its dome." सौध—a mansion with white-washed walls. कुतस्त्यं—whence comes this. हंसतूल॰—.a silken bed stuffed with downy feathers; see p. 97. शीतरश्मि॰—the nymphs are supposed to be borne along the ropes formed of the lunar rays. देववि &c.—like the goddess Lakshmî. शारदशशाङ्क—is peculiarly white, and is often referred to by Sanskrit poets; of. Śâk. कः शारदीं ज्योत्स्नां पटान्तेन वारयति III. p. 74.

 संवाह्यमाना—(1) being acted upon; (2) gently pressed or shampooed. सङ्कुचति—(1) is closed; (2) closes her eyes in sleep; this is one of the signs which distinguish her from the immortals. निद्राति v. l .— sleeps. शबलित—variegated, decked. उद्भिन्न—on which there appear. निर्भर— v. l . दुर्लब makes no sense. The Com's meaning असह्य is far fetched. वैवर्ण्य—change of colour, it being dried up. वर्णक—the paint or unguent. परिभोगा॰— according as they are used or worn. धुसरिमन्—duskiness (which does not appear in the case of heavenly beings). These are the accidents from which the denizens of heaven are exempt. Three of the signs here mentioned are among the six by which Damayantî recognized Nala; cf. सुरेषु नापश्यदवैक्षताक्ष्णोर्निमेषमुर्वीभृति सम्मुखी सा। इह त्वमागत्य नले मिलेति सञ्ज्ञानदानादिव भाषमाणम्॥ नाबुद्ध बाला विबुधेषु तेषु क्षोदं क्षितेरैक्षत नैषधे तु। पत्ये सृजन्त्याः परिरम्भमुर्व्याः सम्भूतसम्भेदमसंशयं सा॥ स्वेदः स्वदेहस्य वियोगतापं निर्वापयिष्यन्निव संसिसृक्षो। हीराङ्कुरश्चारुणि हेमनीव नले तयाऽऽलोकि न दैवतेषु॥ Nai XIV. 19-21 तरेषा &c—By observing these signs he determines that she is a mortal, and not a superhuman being सौकुमार्य—loveliness. संहताः—joined together, stout and firm (and not loosened like those of married ladies). पाण्डुता॰—mingled with paleness (brought on by her longing, and indicating that she was unmarried yet). Dissolve पाण्डुतया अनुविद्धा. अधरमणि—her ruby-like lower lip अनत्यापूर्ण—not much rounded, not very fleshy. कठोर—devel oped, expanded; hence, blooming.

 P. 140. अनङ्ग्बाण॰—Because she had not yet been subject to the influence of love. विश्रब्धमधुरं—gracefully or charmingly on account of innocence or ease of mind (freedom from any misgiving). निर्दय॰—cruel, i. e. hard-pressing.अनति क्रान्त॰— ०मर्यादं चेतो यस्य whose mind has never entertained or harboured a thought unworthy of an honourable man. The intended sense is well brought out in the Com.आसक्त्यनुरूपं—Lit. in a manner becoming the love I feel for her; hence, in obedience to the inspiration of love. स्पष्टं—undoubtedly. Cf. p. 98, 1 11. अथाहं— and yet. स्पृष्टास्पृष्टं— just touching her body (neither in close contact with, nor separate from, her person) आविध्द॰—In a manner filled with love and fear blended to-gether; overpowered by the joint influence of. लक्ष्यसुप्तः—feigning sleep; outwardly asleep but observing what passed around. लक्ष्य pretence, shame; cf. इदानीं परीक्षे किं लक्ष्यसुप्तमुत परमार्थसुप्तमिदं द्वन्द Mrich. III. p. 78; also दीपं चापि न मर्षयेदभिमुखं स्याल्लक्ष्येसुप्तं यदि Ibid. III. 18. रोमोद्भेद—horripilation caused by the touch is one of the Śáttvika bhávas; जृन्भा is a Vibhâva or external sign of a feeling; मन्थर may better be taken to mean 'dull;' see Bhúshaṇâ. त्वङ्गत्—moving, trembling. तान्त—wearied, languid; p p. p. of तम् . अनसिपक्व—incomplete, broken. अपाङ्गपरभाग &c.—with the beautiful corners of her eyes reddened. वास॰—see com. विलास—particular posture of the body in the presence of a lover; विलासोऽङ्गविशेषो यः प्रियाप्तावासनादिषु. विभ्रम—particular sportive actions of the body under the influence of love; amorous gesticulations. See com. व्यवहितानि—Lit, screened or mixed with; hence, full of. व्रीडान्तराणि—controlled by modesty (i. e. not freely display-ed). कामावेग॰—under the sway of vehement love. साध्यसावास—साध्वसजनित आयासः (Madhy. Comp.); languor or exhaustion. स्वेदपुलक—clusters of the drops of perspiration.

** P. 141. **मधुर॰—charmingly contracted at the corner. विभाग-lit means the third part.' तृतीयो भागस्त्रिभागः। सङ्ख्याशब्दस्य वृत्तिविषये पूरणार्थत्वमिष्यते। यथा शतांशः सहस्रांशः इति। Malli on Kum V. 67. आविष्ट—possessed by. किमपि—under the influence of some unknown power; I do not know how. As to the unknown power, see p. 143. 1. 11. अननुकूल—unpleasant, disagreeable. विप्रलम्भ— an iilusion. माया—magic power by which such illusions are wrought or unreal scenes presented to view. प्रतिशयितो भवामि— I shall lie down without food till I gain my object. Cf. अनया च किलास्मै प्रतिशयिताय स्वप्ने समादिष्टम् P. 149; कुरङ्गैरपि प्रतिशयितैरिव Kâd. p. 835 प्रतिशयन means lying down without food, with an intention even to die, before a deity, and keeping there until one secures the desired end.

 तान्त—withered; see above. क्लिष्ट—worn out. निवसनो॰—Dissolve क्लिष्टं निवसनमुत्तरीयं च यस्याः; because the same garments were used; according to a line quoted by Mr. Agashe, a. wömanin'separation wears—वासस्तदेव वपुषो वलयं तदेव हस्तस्थ सैव जघनस्य च रत्नकाञ्ची, निरलक्तक &c.—drily red, being destitute of the red lac-paint. जर्जरित— parched धूम्र— dusky. रुधिरावशेषं—the animation and glow being gone, only blood remains behind. लोहिततर—excessively red. कुलचारित्र—virtuous conduct war-thy of a noble family; चरित्रमेव चारित्रम्. विभ्रमेण—which had the grace of, which looked like. एकवेणी॰—The mass of hair collect-ed into a single braid and allowed to hang on the back reaching down the hips. Ladies in separation are often described by poets as wearing this एकवेणी; of. नीलभागाभया वेण्या जधनं गतयैकया। नीलया नीरदापाये वनराज्या महीमिव॥ Rám. Sund Ká. 15. 25 etc.; Ayodh. K. X. 9 Śâk. VII. ततः प्रविशति एकवेणीधरा शकुन्तला p. 194; and Śl. 21; गण्डा भोगात्कठिनविषमामेकवेणीं करेण Megh. II. 32. चूडीका—a sort of bodice, a covering. The tech-nical meaning (a bracelet made of the horn of a rhinoceros or the like, i.e. of ivory &c.) given by the Bhú. does not seem to be intended, as is shown by the word परिवृता. पतिव्रतापताका—the banner, i. e. the best of. The preceding signs showed that she was like a dutiful wife, in separation, pining for her husband. क्षामक्षामा—greatly emaciated. The repetition shows excess; of. क्षामक्षामकपोलमाननं Śâk. III. 8. अनुभाव—superhuman power. वर्णावकाश—complexion. वात्सल्य—maternal affection.

** P. 142.** मणिभद्रस्येति—See p. 19. राजराजप्रवर्तितां—See supras p. 127. वो जननी— Being the mother of Arthapála, she bore maternal relation to the other Kuma'ras also, as they were bound together by a sort of artificial fraternity.

 Cf. Wilson's remark on this—"The fifth section relatos the adven-ures of Pramati, who is here represented as the son of Kâmapala by the Yakshiņi Tárávali, and not, as in the Introduction, the son of Sumati, Arthapála is there also called the son of Tara'valt, while, as we have seen in the preceding narrative, he is described as the son of Kántimatî. Either the author has been nodding, or the Introduction is the work of a different writer, who has been heedless or forgetful of the narrative of his predecessor." Such is Wilson's remark ; and the sentence further on कथं मत्सुत एवायं वत्सस्य अर्थपालस्य प्राणभूतः सखा &c. on p. 143, at first sight seems to support it. But it is due to Wilson's ignorance of the fact that Indian ladies regard the friend of their son as a son and address him as such. In the above sentence she calls Pra. her son but calls Artha.वत्स. Even in Nârâyanabhaṭṭa's पूर्वपीटिका of the Daś. published by Mr. Agashe, he along with प्रमति is called the son of Sumati.

 पितुः—i. e. his uncle. ०कलुषिताशया—my mind or reason be. ing clouded; see p. 129, 11. 13, 14. अनुशयविधुर—अनुशय is fr. शी with अनु and means 'what sticks to the mind after (अनु) and causes pain,' hence repentance, remorse. विधुर—विगता धूः कार्यभारो यस्मात् (with अच् added at the end); which cannot bear a burden or responsibility; hence, unnerved, helpless; hence, here, distressed; afflicted with repentance. Cf. मयि च विधुरे भावः कान्ताप्रवृत्तिपराङ्मुखः Vik. IV. 20. रक्षोरूप—in the form of a demon. चण्डिका—a passionate or irascible woman, a virago. प्रवासदुःखाय—that you should experience the pangs of separa-tion. आविष्टा—possessed. श्रावस्ती—name of a town, the modern Sahet-mahal, on the southern bank of the river Irávatí It was the capital of the Uttar-Kosalas, 58 miles north of Ayodhyá, where Lava is said to have reigned and which is called Śarávatî in Ragh. XV. 97. "It was the Savatthipura of the Buddhists. Buddha resided here for 25 years.'' Agashe. न तत्त्वतः परिच्छिन्नः—I did not know you properly or as to your real character. अविरल॰—beset with many, abounding in, dangers. सुभग—pleasant or delightful in summer; Cf सुभगसलिलावगाहाः Śák. I. 3. विमानहर्म्य—a very lofty palace (with seven stories); विमान may be a name, or a building like a balloon in form; cf. विमान–परिच्छिन्द. Vik. II.

** P. 143.** आत्मालीकप्रत्याकलन॰—Adv. comp.; with trepidation seizing me at (the consciousness of my crime) the offence I had offered to my husband. She was afraid because Gaurî might know her crime and reprove her for the same. प्रणत—bowed down with, overpowered. It may mean 'favourably inclined,' if the reading be ०हृदयाम्. प्रत्यापन्न॰—I regained my superhuman power भाव—heart. आत्मानं &c.—do not disclose or communicate to each other their love. आघ्राता—overpowered or subdued (enslaved by). लब्धलक्षः—on finding an opportunity or leisure. Perhaps the reading is ०लक्ष्यः—on definitely knowing the object of his love (lit. his mark). प्राञ्जलिं—प्रबद्धः अञ्जलिर्येन तम्. अभ्यवर्तिषि—set out for. निगम a residence of merchants; market— place.नैगम—a merchant, a citizen. The word occurs in Vik. V. 4. ताम्रचूड—a cock. वृद्धविटः—an old cunning Brâhmaṇa. नारीकेलजातिः—of the cocoanut breed; a large and strong cock. बलाकाजातिः—one of the Balâkâ kind. It is described as long-necked, white, and of great strength. प्रतिविसृष्ट— set up or matched against.

** P. 144. **व्युत्पादित—enlightened, made acquainted with, the real nature of things. उपहस्तिका—A small purse or box (see com.) अतिसंरब्ध—very furious, greatly excited. अनुप्रहार—as each struck the other. स्वपक्ष—the partisans of each. कण्ठीरवरव—lit. the lion's roar, the war-cry, the cry or shout of encouragement. स्ववाट॰—his own part of the country or party; for the Bráh. belonged to the eastern part of the country. वयोविरुध्दं—Because ययोरेव समं वित्तं &c. सत्यर्थे— If there be an occasion for it; see supra, p 135. 1 6. हंसक—an anklet ( orrament for the test); cf. सरित इव सविभ्रमप्रपातप्रणदितहंसकभूषणा विरेजुः। śś. VII. 23 पर्यायण—alternately. निर्वर्णयन्ती—closely marking or observing. सवितर्कम्—वितर्केण सहितं यथा तथा (Avya); surmisingly. अनाकस्मिक—not accidental, not surely a matter of chance; i. e. occasioned by some motive. चिरस्थिति—standing for a long time. The Com. needlessly takes स्थिति in the sense of स्थैर्य. कथासंश्रिता—engaged in conver-sation. देशातिथि—one coming from a different country (and not merely a different village), a foreigner. गुणः—favour.

 P. 145. सम्बाध—a crowd. तद् हर्म्यतलं—that (i. . that was seen by me) surface of the mansion. प्रकामवि॰—very extensive. प्रतिकृति—a portrait. तावतीं भूमिं—that stage in the progress of love, or that intensity of love. व्ययितोन्मादिता—tormented and driven to insanity. उन्माद is one of the ten stages of love; see supra p. 95, note on उदप्राद्यत निर्बन्धपृष्ट—asked with importunity, or insistingly. समर्थ—adequate; lit capable of explaining the whole. सशयाना—suspecting; pres. Р of शी with सम्. व्याजमुप्तां—feigning sleep. उल्लमन्—swel-ling, rising. किलैष स्वप्न—could this be a dream? गमय—allow to pass.निराशङ्क॰—which might enable me to reside in the princesŚ apartment without any apprehension danger. अभ्युपगमय्य—having somehow made her to agree to it, having persuaded her. खर्वट—a village at the foot of a mountain. See, however, note on नगरग्राम eto. p. 157. विश्वमय्य—having atked me to take rost. Cf शकटदास विश्रामय करभकम्। Mud. P. 70.

 P. 146. प्रत्यादेश—Lit. ordering back; setting aside; hence, an exceler, a rival. Cf. प्रत्यादेशा रूपगर्वितायाः श्रियः Vik. I.; प्रत्यादेशो धनुष्मतां Kâd. P. 5. It also means 'rejec-tion; cf. Śák. VI. 9. नवमालिका—is noted for its softness. Cf. येन नवमालिकाकुसुमपेलवा त्वं &c. śák. p. 19. समापत्तिदृष्टा—reen accidentally. Cf. समापतिदृष्टेन केशिना Vik. p. 10. नारात्र steel-point-edarrows. ०शल्योध्दरणक्षमः—The Com reads ०णाक्षमः,—evidently taking it with the speaker. धन्वन्तरि—The physician of the gods, and one of the 14 jewels produced at the churning of the ocean, with a cup of nectar in his hand परिवर्तित॰—having changed my dress for a woman's; having disguised myself in female apparel नाम—अलीके, assuming the character of, giving out that. The author only knows how a strong and well-built young man with muscular arms like Pramati (all the Kumâras were such) could assume the disguise of a young girl and pass for such. The device, however, is ingeniously planned. Perhaps this part of the story was suggested by the 15th story of Vetâla, wherein mention is made of a boy disguised as a girl and his introduction as such in the royal household and his intrigue with the princess; the following extract from the Kathásaritságara, XII. 22, will make the similarity clear—तद्रपं तं समादाय गत्वा धूर्ताधिपोऽथ सः। तत्प्रियाजनकं भूपमास्थाने तं व्यजिज्ञपत्॥ राजन्नेकोऽस्ति मे पुत्रः कन्या दूगच्च तत्कृते। मयैषा याचिताऽऽनीता स च क्वापि गतोऽधुना॥ तमन्वेष्टुमहं यामि तदेवा रक्ष्यत। त्वया। आनयामि सुतं यावत्त्वं हि विश्वस्य रक्षिता॥ तच्छ्रुत्वा शापभीत्या च प्रतिपद्य स भूपतिः। सुतामानाययामास यशःकेतुः शशिप्रभाम्॥ जगाद चैत पुत्रीं मां कन्यां रक्षेः स्वमन्दिरे। स्वपार्श्व एव चाहारशय्यां चास्याः प्रकल्पयेः॥ Ślokas 27-31; अथ तत्र तया साकं स मन्त्रिसुतभार्यया। तस्थौ द्विजो दिवा नारी रात्रौ च पुरुषोऽभवत्॥९८॥ शशिनाऽनुगतः सख्या तरुणद्विजरूपिणा। आगत्य तं यशः—केतुं प्रह्णो राजानमब्रवीत्॥ आनीतोऽयं मया पुत्रो देहि मे तां स्नुषामित॥ \* \* \* \* ब्रह्मन्न जाने क्व गता सा स्नुषा ते क्षमस्व तत्। अपराधात्सुतस्यार्थे ददामि स्वसुतां तत्र॥ १०३॥ &c. धर्मासन—judgement-seat, throne of justice, i. e. sitting on which a king dispenses judgement. विद्यामयं &c.—The only price charged was learning. वैवाह्य—fit for a marriage-connexion. अनुमता दातुं—betrothed. सन्न्यसिष्ये—सन्न्यासं ग्रहीष्यामि (i. c. enter upon the fourth stage of life ). दुरनिरक्षतया—the care of (grown up ) daughters being an arduous or difficult task. आपन्नशरण—the protector or succour of the distressed. अधीतिन—अधीतमनेन proficient in sacred lore. See com. at p. 147. अगति—helpless, having no other course left आदिराज—his predecessors (आदिराजानां &c.); Or ring. आदिराजः, which may refer to Vaivasvata Manu; cf. वैवस्वतो मनुर्नाम, माननीय मनीषिणाम्। आसीन्महीक्षितामाद्यः प्रणवश्छन्दसामिव॥ Ragh. I. 11. ' The chief of those whose careers were as noble and exalted as that of Manu, the irst king' आदिराजो मनुरिव प्रजानाम्। Rámá. According to some आदिराज is पृथु. The Mah. - BLár. mon. tions आदिराज as one of tha cons of अविक्षितः—अविक्षितः परिक्षित्तु शबलाश्वश्च वीर्यवान्। आदिराजो विरागश्च शाल्मलिश्च महाबलः॥ A śr. V. P. 101. 40. But this king could not have been meant. Dandin probably refers by आदिराज to the preceding kings, as in his Kávyádarśa:—आदिराजयशोबिम्बमादर्शे प्राप्य वाङ्मयम्। तेषामसन्निधानेऽपि न स्वयं पश्य नश्यति॥—where the Com. saye आदिकालीना इक्ष्वाकुप्रभृतयो राजानः as is also clear from the author's word तेषाम् in the third line. धुर्यः— धुरं वहतीति; also धौरेय by adding ढक् (एय); see com.; lit. ' one who bears a yoke;' hence, the chief of.

** P. 147.** भवद्भुज—gives tho हेतु. Her chastity remaining inviolate under the shadow of your arm. अभिमनायमानः—approving of it. Cf. supra, अभ्यमनायिष्ट चान्तरात्मा p. 138. फल्गुनीषु उत्तरासु—The twelfth lunar mansion consisting of two stars having the figure of a bed. Here the full-moon-day is meant, as this is the asterism of that day and gives the name to the month. गोरुतान्तरं— A distance covered by the lowing of a cow. वानीर— a sort of esne. विहारव्याकुले—being deeply engaged in sport. मग्नापसृतः—diving and swimming off (under water ). जामाता नाम—here नाम is used to indicate & teigned or pretended action ( अलीके ); cf. supra भीतो नामावलत्य p. 132; and infra कार्तान्तिको नाम भूत्वा p. 159. उन्मङ्क्ष्यामि— Ishall emerge. आस्थानी—the royal assembly. श्रीभुजा॰— he is fit to serve you, the enjoyer of wealth, i. e. the wealthy king.गृह्णीतेन्— one who has mastered. Words like अधीतिन्, गृहीतिन् &c., govern the loc. of their object, by the Vārt.' क्तस्येन्विषयस्य कर्मण्युपसङ्ख्यानम्.' The six angas or minor works necessary for the proper understanding of the Vedas and the ritual are:—शिक्षा कल्पो व्याकरणं निरुक्तं ज्योतिषां गतिः। छन्दोविचितिरित्येष षडङ्गो वेद उच्यते॥ आन्वीक्षिकी—logic; it also means metaphysics आत्मविद्या. Cf. Kâmandaka आन्वीक्षिक्यात्मविद्या स्यादक्षिणात्सुखदुःखयोः। ईक्षमाणस्तया तत्त्वं हर्षशोकौ व्युदस्यति॥

 चतुःषष्टिकला &.—For the sake of the curious reader the sixty ..four arts mentioned by Hindu writers are given below in a foot-note from Vâtsyâyana's Káma.—Sút. III. 16.\* आगम—theory. प्रयोग— Practice. तन्त्र—a treatise on a particular art.

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

 \* गीत, वाद्यं, नृत्यं, आलेख्यं, विशेषकच्छेद्यं, तण्डुलक्कसुम बलिविकाराः, पुष्पा स्तरणं, दशनवसनाङ्गरागः, मणिभूमिकाकर्म, शयनरचनं, उदकवाद्यं, उदकाघातः, चित्राश्वयोगाः, माल्यग्रथनाविकल्पाः, शेखरकापीडयाजनं, नेपथ्यप्रयोगाः, कर्ण-पत्रभङ्गाः, गन्धयुक्तिः, भूषणयोजनं, ऐन्द्रजालाः, कौचुमाराश्च योगाः, हस्तलाघवं विचित्रशाकयूष ( ० कापूप॰) भक्ष्यविकारक्रिया, पानकरस-रागासवयोजनं, सूची वानकर्माणि, सूत्रक्रीडा, वीणामरुकवायानि, प्रहें- लिका, प्रतिमाला, दूर्वाचकयोगाः, पुस्तकवाचनं, नाटकाख्यायिकादर्शनं,काव्यसमस्यापूरणं, पट्टिकावित्रवानविकल्पाः, तक्षकर्माणे, तक्षणं, वास्तुविद्या, रूप्य-रत्नपरीक्षा, धातुवादः, मणिरागाकरज्ञानं, वृक्षायुर्वेदयोगाः, मेषकुक्कुटलावयुद्ध-

 पुराण—A Purána treats of five topics—सर्गश्च प्रतिसर्गश्च वंशो मन्वन्तराणि च। वंशानुचरितं चैव पुराणं पञ्चलक्षणम्॥. इतिहास—fr. इतिह a particle expressing past occurrences orally handed down; तदास्ते अस्मिन्; properly means the philosophy of history ; his torical facts so arranged as to teach moral lessons &c. It is defined as धर्मार्थकाममोक्षाणामुपदेशसमन्वितम्। पूर्ववृत्तं कथायुक्तमितिहासं प्रचक्षते॥ आख्यायिका—see supra, p. 21 उपनिषदः—portions of the Veda ( forming the उत्तरमीमांसा or वेदान्त ) the main object of which is to explain the true relation of the world, as also of the individual soul, to Brahma, and to point out thereby the true means of salvation. अर्थशास्त्र—or the Pûrvamîmámsâ, which explains the Vedic Mantras applicable to the ritual. Or अर्थशास्त्र may have its usual meaning of ' The science of wealth or political economy.' निर्मत्सरः &c.—an appreciator of the merits of others without being jealous.

 ** P.. 148. शक्यः—** of agreeable or sweet address. संविभाग॰—munificient. श्रुतधरः—श्रुतस्य धरः, धरतीति धरः, श्रुत sound knowledge of the Śástras; of श्रुतमप्यधिगम्य ये रिपून् &c. Kir. II. 41. न च गुणेषु &c.—Nor do I see any virtue which he does not possess. वैलक्ष्य—embarrassment, perplexity, confusion. अनित्यता॰—by mentioning the fact that worldly things are after all transitory. उपक्रमिष्यसे—proceed to, be prepared for. अनूनैः—of great value. उपच्छन्द्य—having coaxed or gratified. कपटप्रपञ्च—the laying ont or formulating a fraudulent plan or scheme. पाञ्चालशर्मा—This somewhat abrupt introduction of the name of the Brâhmaņa at the close of the episode is probably for the sort of alliteration it gives with पञ्च in प्रपञ्च. निपुणं—cleverly, skilfully. अहीनकाल— immediate, without loss of time. नवमालिकां—The princess of that name; also, the creeper of that name. स्मितमुकुलित—blooming with a smile. ऊर्जितं—high spirit, nobility of mind. This may also be taken as an adj. meaning "lofty', noble, qualifying चेष्टितम्.

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

विधिः, शुकशारिकापलापन, उत्सादने संवाहने केशमर्दने च कौशलम्, अक्षरमुष्टिकाकथनं, म्लेच्छित विकल्पाः, देशभाषाविज्ञानं, पुष्पशकटिका निमित्तज्ञान, यन्त्रमातृका, धारणमातृका, सम्पाठ्यं मानसी, काव्यक्रिया, अभिधानकोषः, छन्दोज्ञानं, क्रियाकरूपः, छलितकयोगाः, वस्त्रगोपनानि, द्यूतविशेषाः, आकर्षक्रीडा, बालक्रीडनकानि, वैनायिकीनां वैजयिकीनां व्यायामिकीनां च विद्यानां ज्ञानम्, इति चतुःषष्टिरनविद्याः। शाखान्तरे चतुःषष्टिमूलकला उक्तास्तासां कर्माश्रयाश्चतुर्विंशतिः, द्युताश्रया विंशतिः शयनोपचारिकाः षोडश, पतस्र उत्तर कच्छा। For there see the com. on the Kâma-Sûtras,

 **P. 149**. सुहोषु—The Suhma country lay to the west of Vanga (Eastern Bengal). Its capital ताम्र लिप्त (also called दामलिप्त ताम्रलिप्त &c. ) is identified with the modern Tumlook on the right bank of the Cossye (the Kapiśá of Kálidâsa ). In ancient times the town was situated nearer the sea and was a place of considerable maritime trade. परिवादीनी—a seven stringed instrument, a guitar. विस्मृत॰—adv. ; in a manner forgetting her passion for her residence on Vindhya. विन्ध्यवासिनी—the goddess Durgâ (as the goddess of the Vindhya Mountain), called further on Somápîḍá. "A celebrated temple to her under this appellation is in the neighbourhood Mirzapur. "-Wilson. प्रतिशायिताय—see supra, p. 141. 1. 8. अनुजीविष्यति—will be the vassal of, will depend on. कन्दुकनृत्य—playing at ball, moving the ball dexterously;see p. 151. 11 9-16. The commentator's कन्दुकवन्नृत्यं तेन does not seem to be right. कन्दुकावती—the final vowel of a word is lengthened before मत् or वत् when it contains many vowels and is not a word of the अजिरादि class and the whole forms a name; see Pán. VI. 3. 119 quoted in the com. ; thus; अमरावति but वलयवती (which is not a name). धात्रेयिका—hre foster-sister; see com. The aff is ढकू ( ष्य ), धात्रेयः m. , धात्रेयिका (also धात्रेयी ) fem.

** P. 150.** बलवत्—forcibly; often used an adv. नूपुरक्वणिसं—the tinkling of anklets. मेजीवितं—i e. his beloved. निरुष्मता— (1) coldness; (2) want of animation, spiritlessness. सुदृष्टं करयित्वा— making her take a full view of me. निष्प्रतिक्रियान्—power less to retaliate, which find no means of retaliation. वेशदास—alave of a harlot. वेशः—the courtesan-class. समर्थयेयं—I shall be upholding or confirming. अध्वदर्शी—I will myself lead the way. उदैरत—Imperfect 3rd pere. pl. ईर् 2 Á. to go, with उद् अनिषिध्ददर्शना—a sight of her is freely allowed to all. This shows that in Duṇḍin's time also, princesses and high- class ladies did not expose themselves to public eyes except on occasions.

 ** P. 151.** सकाशवर्तिनी—I will stand at her elbow. रङ्गपीठ—any seat for beginning any exhibition. अतिष्ठत्—she at once atood in (i. s. occupied, took possession of) my heart. अन्तराले in her passage from where she stood to my heart. चित्रीया—admiration. A noun from the denom. verb चित्यर्ती derived from चित्र by affixing क्यच् (य ). Toe verbs thus formed are A tm. च द्विज हिं &c. —He means that she is superior even to Lakshmi ın beauty, excellence &c. **अनवद्य—**a double negative; वद्य is what is fit to be spoken; अवद्य is what is fit to be condemned,or blameable; and न अवद्यमनवद्यं what is faultless, praiseworthy excellent. Lakshmî is वद्या because she submits to the embraces of many. **अयातयाम—**यातो यामः उपभोगकालो यस्य; fresh, not previously enjoyed by any one. For यातयाम comp. Bg. XVII. 10. अनघ—unblemished; blameless, excellent, faultless; without blemish or deformity. व्यत्यस्त— thrown crosswise; or, with the palms turned downwards. अमन्दराग॰—the spots on which were of a deep-red paint. अक्षि in the case of the ball means 'eye-like spots. ' In the other case अमन्दराग means 'excessive or intense passion.' लीलाशिथिलं—gently (lit. loosely, not with great force) through sportiveness. समाहत्य—striking it down. हस्तपृष्ठेन &c. —Throwing it up after having caught it on the back of her hand. चदुलदृष्टिलाञ्छित—marked with her quickly-moving glances. A series of glances is often compared, on account of the dark pupil being prominent therein, to a garland of blue lotuses by Sanskrit poets. भ्रमरमाला॰—The row of bees corresponds to the series of her glances. मध्यविलभ्बित &c. —Striking it with greater or less force; keeping the time of its rapid, slow or ordinary motion. चूर्णपद—has a technical meaning; it means—stepping backwards with a degree of rapidity propor- tionate to the motion of the ball and impelling it with propor- tionate force. पक्षं &c. —She kept it fluttering in space like a bird, hitting it alternately with her right or left hand as it approached her obliquely or in a straight line. दुरोत्थितं &c. —When it rose to a great height, she struck it in its rapid descent and took the musical leap, i. e. sprang forward ten steps to catch it.

 **P. 152.** **प्रत्यागमयत्—**brought it back to its original or starting point. **अनेककरणमधुरं—**gracefully on account of the several feats of dexterity. उच्चावचाः—loud and gentle, manifold. प्रशंसावाचः—exclamations of applause. तत्क्षणा॰—In whom I came to confide the very moment. The Com. reads प्रतिक्षणारुढविभ्रमं—whose admiration grew every moment. If this be the original reading, it must be taken as an adv along with कण्टकितगण्डं &c. ; or it will be better to read प्रतिक्षणारूढविभ्रमे qualifying मयि. But the reading in the text is better. उत्फुल्लेक्षणं—with my eyes dilated. मयि—is तस्या अभिमुखीभूय तिष्ठति मयि. तत्प्रथमावतीर्ण &c. —under the influence of love felt then for the first time. तदनुमार्ग—following the motion of the ball.
**मद्दर्शनलज्जया—**abashed by my gaze **पुष्पमयं** &c. —a flowery cage was as it were formed by the red- spottted ball whirled about her in rapid rotations. पञ्चबिन्दुप्रसृत—giving five strokes to the ball rapidly so as to make it appear like five specks. गोमूत्रिकाप्रचार—a movement like the undulating line of forked lightning (Wilson). रागविभ्रम—(1) motion of the different colours; (2) the flashing of its colour. भूषणमणि &c. —in which the jewels in her ornaments jingled in correspondence with the fallings of her foot-steps. अपदेशस्मित— smiles under some pretext. प्रतिसमाहित— re-adjusted. शिखण्डभार–mass of her hair, abundant tresses. **समाघट्टित—**struck together. **अञ्चितोत्थित—**beautifully rising. **वेल्लित—**waved आवर्जित॰—in which her long arms were converged. त्रिक—
the distance between the shoulder-blades. अवगलित &c. —in which the golden sockets of her displaced ear-ornaments were put back with such quickness that her ball-sport was interrupted. **असकृदूत्क्षिप्यमाण॰—**in which the ball was whirled in circles round and within (the space of) the hands and feet lifted up.

 **P. 153.** ०मध्ययष्टिकम्—In which the very thin (lit. stick-like) middle part of her body was now seen and now lost to viow. **विपर्यस्त**—toseed about. अङ्कुरित—appearing as drops. दूषित—wetted. पत्रभङ्ग—The ornamental paintings on. **श्रवणपल्लव—**The tender leaf worn on the ear as an ornament. **नियमन—**restraining, adjusting or keeping in position. निमील्य—bending, shrinking. उन्मील्य—unbending, stretching forth. एकेनैवानेकेनेव—Cf. for the idea एकोऽपि त्रय इव भाति कन्दुकोऽयं &c. quoted in the Introduction. सानुराग—(1) full of love; (2) affectionate. कुवलय॰—The blue lotus or नीलोत्पल is one of the five arrows of Oupid. सापदेशं—under some pretext; one of the ways in which Sanskrit poets make maidens betray their love. Cf. दर्भाङ्कुरेण चरणः क्षत इत्यकाण्डे **&c. Ś ák. II. 13;** and यदियं पुनरप्यपाङ्गनेत्रा परिवृत्तार्धमुखी मयाऽद्य दृष्टा Vik. I. 18. यत्नवत् —with care. अत्युदारं—splendid, liberal. प्रणयपेशल—gracefully through love. आशास्यं —merely to be wished for (i. e. not to be realized). विरक्तः—disgusted with or ceasing to love her.

 **P. 154.** रोषादिव—Because she had fixed her affection on one who had excelled him (Káma) in beauty. शम्बरद्विषा—By Káma. Śambara was demon killed by Pradyumna, son of Krishna, and supposed to be Káma re-born as such; hence शम्बर॰ means Káma. **विदितभा**०—By whom the state of her mind i. e. that she was affected by love) was known. विधिः—a thing ordained, or course of events. **बहु विकल्पयतोः**—who discussed the matter, expressing many misgivings. नियम—morning devotions. अनुकूल—calculated to please, agreeable. **उपकार्या—**a tent. उपरूध्द॰—held fast or bound by. ह्तायाः &c.—that hussy, Chandrasená.जालरन्ध्र—the lattice-holes तच्चेष्टा॰—appointed to watch her actions. वराकी—the wretch. किल—as is said; or, as I hear. This shows that he treated the prophecy with scorn.

 ** P. 155.** स्पन्दमानः—swimming. प्रत्युषसि—the next morning. वाहनं—A boat or ship; of प्रलयपयोधिजले धृतवानसि वेदं विहितवहित्रचरित्रमखेदम्। Gîta यवनाः—Wilson says—"By Yavanas we are here probably to understand Arabs, who, we know, were at this period the chief traders and navigators in the Indian and Ohina seas.” रामेषुनाम्ने—The name Rámeshu seems to be an Indian name; the Yavanas, therefore, were under the command of an Indian navigator. So it appears the Yavanas mentioned here were some settlers from Arabia on the sea-coast near the Suhma country and mixed up with the Indian fishermen there. नाविकनायक—the captain. अपि—it is possible that. द्राक्षाणां &c.—It seems it was their trade to capture men and sell them as slaves to work in large fields of grape-plants or vine- yards. मद्गु—a galley or war-ship. सम्प्रहार—a hand-to-hand fight. पराजायिषत—Pass. Aor. 3rd pers. pl. of जि with परा. अगतीन्— helpless. अवसीदतः—giving way, whose courage failed them. अवसादय्—to rout, to destroy. भल्ल —a large arrow with a cres- cent-shaped blade at the end. अनभिसर—who was unattended or without servants; it may also mean, who had not come forth to fight. सांयात्रिकाः—my comrades in the fight. The word properly means a ‘merchant trading by sea ;' समुदितानां गमनं द्वीपान्तरगमनं वा संयात्रा। सा प्रयोजनमस्य; संयात्रा + ठञ् ( इक ). कृतान्तविल॰—The evil pastimes of fortune or fate; for this meaning of कृतान्त of. p. **127, 1. 14.** हर्षकिलकिला॰—peculiar shouts of rejoicings. किल किला is an onomatopoetie word. Cf. **संसक्ताकुलकेलयः** किलकिलाकोलाहलैः सम्मदात् **&c. Mál.- Mádh. V. 11.**

 ** P. 156**. दुर्वारा—Difficult to be managed or properly steered. **दुर्वहा **V. l.—difficult to be controlled or guided, **अननुकूल—**contrary, not favourable गाढपातित॰—having dropped the stone-anchor as that it should stick fast to the ground This is an adverbial comp. नितम्बभाग— the middle region or the skirts. गन्धपाषाण—minerals, such as red arsenic and others; or, benzoin (शैलेय). उपत्यका— adjoining ground. We should rather expect अधित्यका (table-land) here, first because he is referring to the land above the नितम्ब of the mountain, and also because such minerals are found on the tops of mountains. Cf. अधित्यकायामिव धातुमय्यां Ragh. **II. 29. चन्द्रकोत्तरं—**marked by the spreading circles caused by the drops of &c. गोत्रवारि—water of a mountain-stream. **मञ्जुलतर**—exceedingly lovely or beautiful, engaging, आभोग—expanse, avenue. अतृप्ततरया—that never got satisfied; that was the more thirsty. पश्यन्—enjoy. ing the sight of; attracted by. शोणीभूत—made red. नालीक—a lotus. भीमरूप—of hideous aspect. निर्भर्त्सयता—menacingly; the root, which is A'tm. , is often used in the Paras, by our poet. चित्रग्रावाणं—having, or covered with, (mineral) stones of varie- gated colours. प्रियहिताय—for happiness and benefit. **सङ्कल्प—**determination, resolute purpose,

 ** P 157.** प्रमाण—Proofs. त्रिगर्तः—A most arid country in ancient times. It stood for the desert on the east of the Śatadru and included the tract between the Satlej and the Sarasvatî, containing Ludhiana and Patiala on the north and some portion of the desert on the south. स्फीत—large. सार—property, estate. दशशताक्षः—Indra (the god with a thousand eyes), the dispenser of rain. **नववर्ष दश**o is idiomatic for 'there was no rain.' ओषध्यः—ओषः पाकः धीयते आसु इति; annual plants or herbs that die after bearing fruit. See com. , and Manu. **I. 46. वनस्पतयः—**large trees that bear fruit without apparent blossoms; see note on the word at p. **137.** क्लिबाः—without watery substance, empty. स्त्रवन्ती—a river. निस्यन्द—the current. उत्स—a stream. The word occurs in the Veda in the same sense. कथा—jovial talks, merry conversations. शुष्क—lean, starving. नगरग्राम॰—नगर is the principal town of a province where a king resides; cf. Ragh. **IX. 2**. खर्वट is a small town having a market-place, and graded between a town and a village. यत्रैकतो भवेद् ग्रामो नगरं चैकतः स्मृतम्। मिश्रं तु खर्वट नाम नदीगिरिसमाश्रयम्॥ quoted by Bhánujî Dîkshita in his com. on Amara. **III. 5. 33.** Kshîrasvâmî says खर्वटश्चतुःशतग्रामाणां सङ्ग्रहस्थानं, but there seems to be some inaccuracy here; for, from Daṇḍin's use of it, it does not appear to be such a big thing (of Marathi खेडें which is derived from it ) **पुटभेदन—**a** **town; it is given in Amara. as a synonym of नगरी; it is variously derived as—पुटानि भाण्डानि भिद्यन्तेऽस्मिन्; पुटा भाण्डवासनानि भिद्यन्ते अस्मिन्; or पु अश्चखुरैर्भिद्यते इति, according to some. Mr. A'gáshe observes, "These terms are found in Kauțilîya **II. 1. 19,** and they are explained there in a foot-note as under—नगरं राजधानी, क्षुल्लकपाकारवेष्टितं खर्वटम्। पट्टनं शकटैर्गम्यं घाटिकैनौर्भिरेवच। नौभिरेव तु यद्गम्यं पट्टणं तत्प्रचक्षते। द्रोणमुखं जलनिर्गमप्रवेशं (where exit or entrance is by means of boats) पट्टणमित्यर्थः। इति रायसपेणीसूत्रव्याख्याने। **प. २०६.** नगराणि करवर्जितानि निगमवणिजां स्थानानि.......खर्वटानि कुनगराणि (small towns)। पत्तनानि जलस्थलपथयोरन्यतरयुक्तानि। इति प्रश्नव्याकरणसूत्रव्याख्याने। **प. ३०६.**

 **अजाविक—**flooks of sheep and goats; अजाश्च अविकाश्च तेषां समाहारः, क is added स्वार्थे to अवि; see also com. अभाविकटं v. l. here. कट is the affix in the sense of सङ्घात; see com. (Várt. on सम्प्रोदश्च कटच्। Pág. **V. 2. 29**). गवल—a wild buffalo. उद्वहन्—bearing her on his back. स्वमांसासृगपनीत etc.—Daṇḍin seems to have taken the principal part of this story from Guṇádhya's Brihatkathá; cf. with this the story of a merchant's son related by Gomukha to Naraváhanadatta in Kathás. Lambaka **X.** विश्चेष्टमान—rolling, struggling. आर्द्राशयः—with his heart melted with pity. गहनोद्देश—a region in the depth of the forest. रोपित—healed. इङ्गुदीतैला॰ —The oil of Iñigudî is said to have this virtue. Cf. व्रणविरोपणमिङ्गुदीनां तैलं न्यषिच्यत मुखे &c. Śâk. **IV. 14.**

 **P. 158. उद्रिक्तधातुम्**—when there was an abundance of vital fluids in his body. उपातिष्ठत्— should rather be A tm उदकाभ्यर्थिनं—as he asked for water to drink. उदञ्चन—a bucket, a pot for drawing water out of a well. प्रणुनोद—pushed him in विकल—the cripple, the mutilated man स्कन्धेनोदुह्य—Cf. तद्भार्या... बभ्रामेतस्ततो जारं रुण्डं पृष्ठेऽधिरोप्य तम्। Kathás. **X. 9.28** प्रतीति—fame; for a different meaning, see p. **82, 1. 11.** अवन्तिराजा॰—in the Kathás. the son of the merchant himself becomes the king, himself recognizes her, and punishes her. भूति—riches, wealth. चित्रवध—See supra, p.**591.** 3. विकलीकृतोऽभिमतः—accepted or set down as maimed by me. पापमाचक्षीत—will accuse me of the orimo. अदिनम्—boldly. दर्शितेऽमुष्मिन्स—is not to idio- matic as दर्शितः स विकलः आर्यबुद्धिः—having noble thoughts, noble-minded श्वभ्यः पाचिका—a cook for dogs; i. degraded her.

 ** P. 159. अनुयुक्तः**—questioned. द्रविडेषु—Dravida lay to the south of the wild tract between the Krishņá and the Polar. In its wider sense it included the whole of the Coromandel coast to the south of the Godâvarî. In its stricter sense it must not have extended beyond the Kâverî. Its capital was Káñchî, the same as Conjeeveram, situated on the Vegavatî river, 42 miles south-west of Madras, अननुगुण—lit, having no corresponding merits, unworthy. **यादृच्छिकीं सम्पत्तिं—**the wealth of virtues, excellence, accidently found (i.e. which may or may not be found). अनभिसमीक्ष्य— without approving of, disregaring. **कार्तान्तिकी नाम भूत्वा—**assuming** **the character of an astrologer; feigning to be a fortune-teller. लक्षणवती—endowed with auspicious marks. **सवर्णा—** of his own class. गुणवत्—excellent, well-prepared. शिबिषु—The region on the southern bank of the Káverî; see com. It has nothing to do with the country of Śibi, the famous king of that name, situated near Gândhára in the north. सह पितृभ्याम्—She bad lost her parents as well ag her great fortane. भवनसार—the valuables in the house. संसक्त—riveted. विकट—deformed; hideous; from वि + कट (च) **सृजावन्तः—**possessed of purity, i.e. fair-complexioned, clean-skinned. रक्त॰—This is an auspicious sign indicative of great good fortune; see the quotation from Brihatsam. 70, given on the next page. अव—a linear mark on the fingers of the hand resembling a barley-corn and indicating, according to its position, wealth, progeny, good fortune &c. सम्—even, fleshy, covered with flesh. अशिरालौ—prominent veins are supposed to be a mark of ugliness. अनुपूर्वदृत—tapering. Cf. वृत्तानुपूर्वे च न चातिदीर्घे जङ्घे &c. Kum. I. 35. दुरुपलक्ष्ये—Because they came in close contact with the plump thighs. सकृद्विभक्तः—evenly divided. चतुरस्त्र—symmetrical; mark the comp. चतस्त्र; अस्त्रयो यस्य. The final इ is replaced by अ by the rule सुप्रात...चतुरस्र &. Pán. **V. 4. 120. **बभूव** **तस्याश्चतुरस्रशोभि वपुर्विभक्तं नवयौवनेन। Kum **I. 32.**

 ** P. 160.** ककुन्दर—the cavities of the loins. रथाङ्गाकार—of the form of a wheel; of. रथाङ्गश्रोणिबिम्बया Vik . IV. 18.** **ईषन्निम्र—A little depressed वलित्रयेण—the वलित्रय is gen-erally described in connexion with beautiful ladies; of. supra, p. **44, 1. 9.** उरोभागव्यापिनौ—large enough to cover the whole bosom. विशालारम्भ—their great expanse. स्निग्ध—glossy. उदग्र—prominent. नखमणि—jewel-like, red nails. सन्नत—well-turned. कम्बु &c.—कम्बुवद्वृत्ता बन्धुरा ( curved ) च. वृत—pouting. असङ्क्षिप्त—not contracted or small (with the chin, elegant and not small ). असङ्गत—not joined. अनुवक्र—beautifully अनतिप्रौढ॰—not much developed (i.e. the bud of the tila flower). असित—dark. मधुराधीरसञ्चार—beautifully and swiftly rolling. मन्थर—dull; or, large. इन्दुशकल— i. e. the half-moon on the eighth day of a paksha; अष्टमीचन्द्रशकलाकारं ललाटदेशम् &c. Kád. **p. 31**. श्रवणपाशः—beautiful ears; here पाश signifies beauty or admiration; see supra, **p. 97 1. 9.** अनतिभङ्गुर—not much curling. एकैकनिसर्गसम—each growing by itself and evenly (i. e. there was по branching of the hair). सेयमाकृतिः &c. —Her character must be in keeping with the grace or outward beauty of her form. Cf. न ह्याकृतिः सुसदृशं विजहाति वृत्तम्। Mrich. **IX, 16**; न तादृशा आकृतिविशेषा गुणविरोधिनो भवन्ति। Śák. **IV. p. 86**; यत्राकृतिस्तत्र गुणा वसन्ति। Varáh Brihatsam. 70. 23; यत्राकारस्ततो गुणाः। Agnip. **244 6;** आकृतिमनुगृह्णन्ति गुणाः Viddhaśálabh.

 **P. 161**. अविमृश्य॰—acting thoughtlessly or precipitately. of विवेकभ्रष्टानां भवति विनिपातः शतमुखः Bh. Nît. 10; and अतिरभसकृतानां कर्मणामाविपत्तेर्भवति हृदयदाही शल्यतुल्यो विपाकः। Nît. 105. अनुशय— repentance, regret साकूतं—meaningly, inquiringly. अलिन्द—a dais or raised place near a door. पादशौच—water for the feet. गन्धशाली—गन्धविशिष्टा शालयः; fragrant rice. सङ्क्षुद्य—having gently or carefully pounded. मात्रया—a little. स्थिर—firm. नाली—a kind of pestle. अखण्डैः—not bruised or broken. अर्थिनः—stand in need of. मृजा—cleaning, polishing. शराव—an earthen platter. हत्तान—expanded or spread out. ककुभोलूखल—a mortar of the Arjuna tree. ०मध्यतानवेन—Because a pestle is usually grasped by the middle, which part is therefore worn out. तानव—slenderness.

 **P. 162.** चतुरललि॰—raising it up gracefully and cleverly &c. शूर्पशोधीत &c. —she removed the grains of dust the awn by winnowing them with a basket. **क्वथितपञ्चगुणे—**heated to the boiling point and five times in quantity. **दत्तचुल्लीपूजा—**a custom still observed in many places. The cooking woman throws a few grains into the fire before pouring the rice into the boiling water or placing the vessel containing rice on the hearth. "So Jewish bakers put a little of the dough into the oven. Prof. Peterson. मुकुलावस्था &c. —When the rice was passing the state of a bud, i. e. was moderately swollen. अन्नमण्ड—the scum. दर्वी—a ladle. अवघट्टय—having struck परिवर्त्य—having turned them up and down, आमलक—myrobalans, चिञ्चा—tamarind. उपदंश—condiments. दत्ताङ्गारधूप॰—having scented it with perfumes evaporated on the charcoal. लक्ष्ण॰—finely pulverized. ०तैलामलकः—Rubbing the body with oil and powdered. myrobalans gives a glossy appearance to the skin. त्रिभाग॰—the third part from the upper end of which was out off; तृतीयो भाग त्रिभागः शेषः तत्र लूनस्य अभिमृशन्—touching. पेया—water-gruel mixed with some boiled rice; see Vágbhața quated in the Com. प्रल्कीन्न॰—his whole body sweating. सर्पिर्मात्रा—a little ghee. **सूप—**sauce.

 ** P. 163.** विजातक—cinnamon oil; or rather, dry ginger, black pepper and long pepper. कालशेय—curds churned with a handle without water. अन्धस्—food भृङ्गार—a kind of jug or vase (**झारी** in Marathi) **अगुरु**—agallochum. **ग्रथितसौरभं—**whose fragrance was mixed with. हिमशिशिर—cool like snow. The sl. क्रान्तकान्त etc. quoted in the com, from the Śiśupálavadha **(X. 3)** is meant simply to illustrate how one drink can gratify all the organs of senses. It is the wine that gratifies all the sense there, while in our passage it is the cool fragrant water served out to the merchant that gratifies all his senses. The author himself describes how it did that. करालित— fringed with. कर्कश—rough or rugged. **परिमलप्रवाहोत्पीड—**strong current or flow of the fragrance. परिमलप्रवाल v. l. —परिमलस्य प्रवालाः offshoots of, ie the spreading fragrance. **माधुर्यप्रकर्ष**०—his tongue being quite gratified with the excessive sweetness. **अच्छ—**clear. करक—a small water-pot. आचमनं—Âchamana is the drinking of a little water taken into the hollowed palm of the hand, three times, as a religious rite. It is enjoined at the beginning and at the end of any religious rite; it is also necessary after a bath, drinking, sneezing, sleep, meals, walking through a street, and changing clothes; cf. Yáj. Sm. **I. 196** quoted in the com. , and Manu. **V. 145**—सुप्त्वा क्षुत्वा च भुक्त्वा च निष्ठीव्योक्त्वाऽनृतानि च। पीत्वाऽपोऽध्येष्यमाणश्च आचामेत्प्रयतोऽपि सन्॥. कुट्टिम—a pavement उत्तरीयकर्पट—his upper garment. एतदनपेक्षः—regardless of her; growing cold towards her; (this he did, of course, in order to put her virtues to test; he did not really want to be attached to the harlot). अवरोधमकरोत्—introduced her into the harem. तामपि—The force of अपि is this—As a wife with any sense of self-respect, she should have resented this act of her husband and treated the courtesan with jealousy; but as a true Pativratâ she felt it her duty to subordinate personal happiness to that of her husband (यद्भर्तुरेव हितमिच्छति तत्कलत्रं ) and acted friendly towards her. This is also Kálidasa's view of the duties of a co-wife; cf. प्रतिपक्षेणापि पतिं सेवन्ते भर्तृवत्सलाः साध्यः। Mâlav. V. 19. अहीनं—with assiduous care; lit. without any omission (of duty). परिजनं च &c.—Cƒ. 148 भूयिष्ठं भव दक्षिणा परिजने Śák. IV. 18. दाक्षिण्यनिधिः—explains how she won the affection of her servants. कुटुम्ब—i. e. domestic concerns. त्रिवर्ग—viz. Dharma, Artha and Káma, for securing which a wife is to be married.

 P. 164. सौराष्ट्र—also called आनर्त, is the modern peninsula of Kathiawar. The ancient Dváraká was its original capital. Valabhî appears to be its next capital, the ruins of which were discovered at Bilbi, 10 miles north-west of Bhavanagar. According to the Bombay Gazetteer (as quoted by Mr. A'gáshe) it was a town in the east of Kathiawar about 25 miles west of Bhavanagar and about 25 miles north of Śatruñjaya. The modern name of the town is Valeh. गुह्यकेन्द्र—Kubera नाविकपति—a ship-owner. मधुमती—name of a town. It may probably be Mathurá, which was called मधुपुरी in ancient times, being the residence of the demon Madhu, whose son Lavaṇa was killed by Śatrughna; see Ragh. XV. 28, and our note ad loc. रभसविघ्नित—obstructed through rashness. तद्गृहागमनं—तदु refers to her; i.e. her father's house. From this it seems that Balabhadra lived in the same town after his mar. riage with रत्नवती, instead of going to his native town. ०अतिवर्ती not listening to. दुर्भगा—an unfortunate woman, one hated by her husband. See note on दौर्भाग्य below. निम्बवती—Nimba is a tree known for its bitterness; hence this means 'sour-tempered.' परिबभूव—insulted her by the nickname of का मे गतिः—' what is to be my fate;' or, what course of life should I pursue ? देवशेष॰—flowers previously offered to deities (who brought to her flowers from an idol). अनुनीय—addressing her words of consolation.—दौर्भाग्यं has a technical meaning here-the state of not being loved or favourably looked upon by the husband; opp. is, सौभाग्यं, for which see supra, note on सुभगमानी p. 92 तस्य—refers to दौर्भाग्य. सुदृष्टा—well or favourably seen by them, and my relatives (—said in contrast with अवज्ञया पश्यति in the last sentence); a favourite of my husband; cf. सुदृष्ट p. 150, 1. 8. आ विरामात्—until I die. आश्राव्यम्—should be disclosed त्वन्निदेशवर्तिमी—ready to act according to your ad. vice. मदधिष्ठिता—led or guided by me उदर्क—result. आकार—such grace of form. ईदृशेन—suob, so amiable (a disposition).

 p. 166. पतिद्रोह—hatred shown by a husband. प्रातिवेश्य—a neighbour. राजान्तरङ्गभावेन—By his being in the confidence of the king. ०रूपावयवा—in form and body. विमानहर्म्य—विशेषेण मान्त्यस्मिन्निति; fr. मा with वि and affix अन (ल्युट्); a mansion having seven stories; cf. नेत्रा नीताः सततगतिना यद्विमानाग्रभूमीः Megh. II. तन्मातृप्रार्थन—i. e. the entreaty of Kanakavatî's mother. क्रीडामत्ता नाम— as if blundering in my sport. भ्रंशयेयम्—I shall drop down अनवस्थः—of loose or irregular conduct, unsteady. विपक्षधन—the property of an enemy. बध्दाञ्जली—adv. 'with folded hands.' साभिलाषं—inspired with love for me. रन्ध्र—an opening; a loop-hole; 'taking advan-tage of this circumstance.' The construction is तेन रन्ध्रेण तमुपश्लिष्योपेत्य... यथा असौ मामादाय &c. उज्ज्वलीकृत्य—having fanned the fire of his love or inflamed his passion. नीरन्ध्र—thick, pitchy.

 P. 166. वार्तामापादयत्—spread or circulated a report. मन्देन—a fool that I was, being a dunce. उपेक्षिता—neglected. अतिवर्तिताः—disregarded. संसृष्टः—united with, living in her company. व्यक्तिः—clearance of; i. e. the matter will be clear. पण्यदासी—a female slave she purchased. उपस्कर—provisions. मूल—capital; lit. the original sum. प्रसादकालोपाo—The reading in the foot-note is perhaps better, being simpler; in the reading as we have it, the word वृत्तान्त is without propriety. निर्बिभेद—promulgated, disclosed दण्डवाहिन्—the mayor, the magistrate. मोषेणापहृत्य—having kidnapped न्यायोढा—law-fully married. प्रणिधि—an emissary. श्रेणीप्रातिभाव्येन—by the security of the association of traders. प्रातिभाव्य is an abstract noun from प्रतिभू 'a security.' भावयतः—who looked upon or believed.

 P. 167. शूरसेन—Name of the country about Mathurá, Kálidása has referred to this country in Ragh. VI. 45. कुलपुत्र—a youth of family; or Śúdra, as the Com. takes it. स्वभुजमात्र॰—who had successfully fought many a duel with his own arms for the sake of his friends. निर्व्यूढ—successfully carried out; cf. निर्व्यूढदुर्वह (दुर्धर) प्रतिज्ञाभारस्य वृकोदरस्य Venî. VI. p. 139 ; निर्वाहः प्रतिपन्नवस्तुषु सतामेतद्धि गोत्रव्रतम्। Bhar. Nît. Misc. Ślokas, Ś1. 13. कर्कश—hard-hearted, cruel men. पाण्डुरा—indicating uneasiness of mind, anxiety. अनसि॰—अनतिपरिभुक्ता अत एव सुभगा. प्रौढता—full growth; her eyes indicate her having passed the age of timidity and bashfulness. एकवेण्यादेः—It is usual with ladies whose husbands are absent not to dress or decorate their hair, &c.; see supra, note on एकवेणीभूतेन p. 141. लक्ष्म चैतत्—It is difficult to know what लक्ष्म is meant here ; according to the Bhûshaṇá it means ‘a nail-mark;' it may also be the mark of a mole. ०नामा—The fom is ०नामा or नाम्री. उन्मनायमानः—being greatly agitated at heart. परिवव्राज—went; परिव्रज् has not got its usual sense here. भार्गव—a fortune-teller. भिक्षानिभेन—under the pretext of begging alms.

 P. 168. श्रमणिका—A Buddhist or Jain female mendicant. A female follower of Śramaṇa or Jina; see Vaijayantî as quot-ed in the com, on निर्ग्रन्थिक p. 87. It is strange that these nuns should be found employed in the settlement of love-mattere. शीलभ्रंश—her falling off, or deviation from, virtuous conduct.उपह्वरे—in secret; or, in a private place. समाधिमास्थाय—having recourse to contemplation or penance; the phrase occurs in Kum. V. 2. घटते—endeavours. क्व घटते—how can it stand to reason ? उदारा—excessive. अदुष्टभावता—inviolate chastity, incorruptibi- lity of character. अप्रतिकृत्य—unless the evil influence exerted by it is counteracted; unless the obstacle interposed by it is removed. छत्रं—secretly. प्रणयकुपिता &c.—pretending to be lovingly angry. उपरि—after this is done; in this sense it occurs in Marathi as उपर. ऊर्जित—noble, strong and good- looking. तावतैव—by doing me that much service. सा तथैव &c.—consenting to do so, she favoured him accordingly. निर्ग्रन्थिका—a female mendicant. See supra, note on निर्ग्रन्थिक at p. 87.

 P. 169. परामृशत्रिव—pretending to rub it gently. आंलिख्य—having made a scratch or cut. सान्द्रत्रासा—in great dismay. दुर्नय—folly, ill-advised step. दत्त्वा पटबन्धनं—having put on a bandage or ligature. सामयापदेशात्—pretending to be ill. अब्रुवाणं—when he refused to tell. निर्बन्धेन— pressingly, imper atively. वणिग्ग्राम—The merchant-guild, the committee of merchants. विश्रामप्रविष्टायां—as I entered for the purpose of repose. अद्यापि—goes with न दृष्टः लुब्धाः—misers, covetous people who do not want to pay the fees. दग्धादग्ध—partially burnt, यद्दच्छया— accidentally. विमर्शे—after deliberation, when the matter was thought over.

 P. 170. शाकिनी—A witch, a female seeking the favour of devils. तस्याः शाकिनीत्वम्—This device of proving Nitambavatî a Śákinî and getting her expelled from her house, used here by the young merchant to gain his object, seems to havo been taken from the Vetâlapanchavimśiká, occurring in the Brihatkathâ. Therein one Buddhiśarîra, the minister and companion of Prince Vajramukuţa, adopts this kind of device to secure to his friend Padmávatî, a damsel of that name. See Kathás. Lambaka XII., Taraṅga 8; cf. तदागमनहृष्टोऽथ मन्त्रिपुत्रो नृपात्मजम्। जगाद हन्त गच्छाद्य पद्मावत्या गृहं निशि॥ ततस्तस्याः सनिद्रायाः शूलेनाङ्कं कटीतटे। दत्त्वाऽग्नितप्तेनादाय तदाभरणसञ्चयम्॥ प्रातर्गत्वा श्मशानं च सोऽभूत्तापसवेषभृत्। स्वैरं राजसुतं तं च विदधे शुद्धरूपिणम्॥ अब्रवीत्तं च गच्छैकमितोऽलङ्करणादिमाम्। मुक्तावलीं समादाय त्वं विक्रेतुमिवापणे॥ \*\* श्रुत्वैतद्विजनं कृत्वा स धूर्तस्तमभाषत। अहं तपस्वी भ्राम्यामि सदाऽरण्येष्वित्तस्ततः॥ सोऽहं दैवादिह प्राप्तः श्मशानेऽत्र स्थितो निशि। अपश्यं योगिनीचक्रं समागतमितस्ततः॥ तन्मध्ये चैकयाऽऽनीय योगिन्या राजपुत्रकः। उद्घाटितहृदम्भोजो भैरवाय निवेदितः॥ अतिप्रवृत्ता च मया क्रुद्धेन जघनस्थले। अङ्किता सा त्रिशूलेन मन्त्रप्रज्वालिताश्रिणा॥ हृता मुक्तावली चेयं तस्याः कण्ठान्मया तदा। सैषाऽद्य तापसानर्हा विक्रेया मम वर्तते॥ ग्रस्तः सुतो मे डाकिन्या तयेत्युत्पन्ननिश्चयः। पितृभ्यां शोच्यमानायाः पुरान्निर्वासनं व्यधात्॥ त्वदावर्जने—to win your affection. असाधारणीकृत्य—as an extra- ordinary (and therefore never-failing) remedy. अकामा—i. e. against her will. नैर्ऋत—a Rákshasa. मस्सम्बन्धी—my friend. समसृज्यत—joined in & duel. अनपेक्षापविद्धा—oast down with- out any regard for her safety. शैलशृङ्गभङ्ग—pieces of the peaks of mountains. अक्षपयेतां— dispatched, destroyed.

 P. 171 मत्प्राणैकवल्लभा—The darling of my heart. तिर्यङ्—squintingly, obliquely. कामरूप—assuming any form at will. स्फ़ुरन्ती—trembling or struggling for release. अवरूढाः—janded. निष्कल—discomited, dejected and pale निष्कलङ्क—free from the contamination of sin. प्राणवत्—goes with चन्द्र॰, who was regarded by him as his very life. अवसरेषु—as at the time when he helped the Yavanas against Bhîmadhanvan. स्मिताभिषिक्त—bathed or steeped in smile.

 P. 172. ललितवल्लभा॰—In the following chapter the poet has accomplished a literary tour de force by making Mantragupta tell his tale without the use of any of the labial letters (including the vowels उ and ऊ). The author here poetically accounts for his hero's inability to use the labial letters. Such literary feats are not rare in Sanskrit literature. Śri ráma's Kamsanidhâna is a longer instance of this kind of feat

 कलिङ्गः—The country lay to the south of Odra or Orissa, and extended to the mouth of the Godâvarî. It is identified with the Northern Oircars. संस्थितजन॰—the ground for burning the dead, a cemetery. धरणिज—a tree. Mark how the author is forced to go in search of such words as संस्थित, धरणिज, &c. for the ordinary words मृत, वृक्ष or पादप, owing to his relf-imposed restriction of avoiding al labials. सरसकिसलय—tender (lit juicy) leaves. कालरामे &c.—The night is compared to a lady, and the mass of darkness to the looks of her hair. चलितरक्षसि—Because it is the it time for goblins and rάkshasas for stalking abroad. नीहार—dew. निशीथ—midnight. साल—a tree. निर्हादि—qualifies रटितं further on; sounding through the branches of the thickly- growing trees.' If the reading be, निर्ह्लादिनि—it must be taken as an adj. qualifyingनिशीथे. नेत्रनिंसिनीं etc.—keeping off (pre-venting the sleep that was kissing my eyes. अनेन दग्ध॰—this accursed magician. रिरंसा—noun in आ from the Desid. base of रम्. दित्सता—prep. of the Desid. of दा. रागेण &c.—overpowered by unbounded passion. खिलीकृतः—Lit. nailed down to one spot, made unable to carry out one's object; hence, prevented from enjoying pleasure. कुत्सितो नरेन्द्रः अणक॰; a cursed practiser of charms, a wicked magician; márk the meaning of अणक. See Pán. II. 1. 54 quoted in the com. सिध्दयन्तरायः—an obstacle in his attain- ment of the desired superhuman powers.

 P. 173. तरलतर―very bright (polished). निष्ठाङ्गाररञः—the dust of charcoal reduced to ashes by complete combustion. तडिल्लताकार॰—bright and tawny like the flashes of lightning. अरण्यचक्र—the circuit or expause of the forest. चञ्चत्—waving, flickering. सिध्दार्थक—white mustard seeds. See supra, P. 118, note on गौरसर्षप. चटचटायित—crackling; lit., which made a chat chat sound;' p. p. p. of the verb चटचटायते—formed by affixing य (क्यष्) to the onomatopoetic word चटचट; see Pân. III. 1. 13. निकृष्टाशय—debased in mind.अस्रजर्जर—broken or choked with tears. रणरणिकागृहीतेन—overpowered by anxiety. ग्लान—withered. शीर्णनहने—the tie of which was broken. शिलाशितेन—whetted on a stone-slab; ०सितेन—white like'—is not a good reading; it will also not do to take it as शिलावदसितेन, as the idea of bright- ness will be left out. चिकर्तिषया— with an intention to cut off.

 P. 174. स्कन्धरन्ध्र—a hollow in the trunk. Cf. the similar seene in Mál-Mádh. V., where Aghoraghanta tries to kill Málatî while Mâdhava
rescues her. निध्याय—having watched or observed. क्षीणाधिः—freed From his mental worry or anxiety. कदर्थन—torture, teasing. अकृत्य—improper acts, abominable deeds; see com. नरकाकः—wretched or cursed man. कारणा—agony; this word has occurred before; see p. 82, 15 शीतेतर—From शीतेतर hot and दीथिति rays; the hot-tayed one, the sun;-देहजः is Yama, son of the sun, and god of death. See com. अणीयस्—trifling. He is underrating his own act. न चेदिदं ने॰—If it is not that you do not approve of this; if you have no objection to do so. अक्लेशार्हा—who ill deserves harsh treatment; who ought to be gently treated. कर्णशेखर—the tip of the ear. The eye looked like a blue lotus placod on the tip of her ear; for she was आयतेक्षणा. अञ्जित—gracefully bent. सलिलचर—A device to avoid the use of मीन or मकर, which contains a labial letter. आनत—curved चिल्लिकालता—her long and bushy eye-brows. The word चि° or चुल्लिका which can somehow be made to have the meaning of an eyebrow' (fr. चिल्ल् or चुल्ल् I. P. to make amorous gestures) is purposely used for to get rid of a labial letter. लीलालसं—gently through sportiveness. रागलज्जा॰—acting under the influence of love and bashfulness.

 P. 175. तिरश्चीन &c.—with the moon-shine of her nails spreading slantingly. साचीकृत—turned aside साचि is a prop meaning 'sidelong,' 'aside.'साची…सरसिजं—adv.; cf. Ragh, VI. 14; Mâlav. IV. 15. लिखन्ती—drawing figures on. लङ्घिना—passing across. हृदयलक्ष्य—the target of the heart. रति सहचर—a periphrasis for Madana, which cannot be used. स्यादायितेन—(going) with the swiftness of स्यदायित is the p.p.p. of the denom, verb स्यदायते fr. स्यद speed, a word irregu-larly formed from स्यन्दू; see Pâṇ. VI. 4. 28, quoted in com.; स्यन्देर्घञि नलोपो वृद्ध्यभावश्च निपात्यते। स्यदो वेगः। अन्यत्र स्यन्दः। Sid.- Kan. The Com. seems to have read दक्षिण for दक्ष which makes no sense. Dissolve—हृदयमेव लक्ष्यं तस्य दलने दक्षः रतिसहचरशरस्तस्य स्यदस्तद्वदाचरतीति तेन।. ०सहचरस्य दयितेन v. l.—beloved of, i. e. friend of, Madana. रागानिल॰—with the waves of the heart's yearnings set in motion by the wind of passion. कन्यागारा॰—your taking a residence in the secret apartment. देहज—stands for मनसिज i. e. Madana. कालायसनिगड—iron fetters. रथचरण—a wheel.

 P. 176. नयेत अकीर्त॰ दशां—,i e. will kill me.निकेतनं—Throughout this section the final म् is printed in the form of an Anusvára, as the author has taken care to avoid its labial sound by beginning the following sentence with a letter other than a labial, as then the म् is to be pronounced like the nasal of the class to which that following letter belongs. चन्द्रशाला—a room on the top of a mansion. करतला॰—by gently shaking them with the palm of the hand. निञ्जशेखर॰—olinging to the tips of the filaments of the flowers forming the garlands worn on their heads. संशयित— confounded with, mixed. ततः कृतान्तेन &c.—and so she escaped death. दत्ता चेयं &c.—Cf. supra p. 119, प्रागपि रागाग्निसाक्षिकं &c. आश्चर्यरत्न—the gem in the form of this wonderful beauty. रत्नशैल— The mountain Meru. स्थिर—firm, रागतरल—(1) sparkling with its colour (lustre); (2) agitated by passion. स्न्रेहनिगल—the chains of love; ल is often substituted for ड in Sanskrit, जायारहित—those separated from their wives. Cf. वियोगिहृदयस्थं मन्मथानलमुज्ज्वलयन् supra p. 42. लालस—eager to drink the honey. लङ्घन—close contact. ग्लान—withered, faded. घन—thickly grown.

 P. 177. राजत्—shining, gay in summer. लीलायिततिलक-making the tilaka flowers serve as sportive marks on the forehead of the forest-sites smiling with flowers. निर्भिद्र &c.—the gold-coloured umbrella in the form of the full-blown Karṇikára flower. दक्षिण॰—stands for the southern (Malaya) breeze सहकार॰—सहकारेषु चञ्चरीकाः bees कलिकाश्च. कालाण्डज—the cuckoo bird. रक्तरक्ताधरा—रक्ताः stirred up with passion, amorous रक्ताधराः—beautiful ladies. सन्नाह—prepara- tion for; ०शालिनी—standing out as the first preparer for the battle of sport. लङ्घितलज्जे in which the modesty of bashful maidens was overcome by the love which then took posses-sion of their heart. दर्दुर—name of a mountain in the south, connected with Malaya. It is that portion of the Gháts which forms the southern boundary of Mysore. Cf. Ragh. IV. 51 (शैलौ मलयदर्दुरौ). The commentator, not remem- hering this, has given a fanciful explanation. अनिलाचार्या—अनिल एवाचार्यः the dancing-master. The wind is often referred to by Sanskrit poets as giving instructions in dancing. अलङ्घनीय—impervious to sun-light. लङ्घित—leapt over, seated upon. आलीढ—touched. तरलतरङ्ग—the heaving waves. क्रीडारस &c.—was deeply engaged in sport. सङ्गत—engaged in. शृङ्गारहेला—amorous sports. अनङ्गसङ्घर्ष—emulation in love-matters. एकतन्त्र—solely under the power of his passion for pleasures. तत्र रन्ध्रे—taking advantage of that weak point आन्ध्र—Name of a country, the same as modern Tailangana. The mouths of the Godávarî were in the possession of the A'ndhras.

 P. 178. गलित—faded, vanished. गात्रकान्ति—the youthful bloom of (the body). निरस्तधैर्य—soil, by the charms of the princess. गररस—a poisonous potion or drink. सन्तिष्ठेत—will die. अनन्यज—Káma. सा का &c.— What will be my future state? How shall I fare? अग्रज—a Bráhmaṇa. किल—As the report goes. निकार—insult, humiliation, offence. दत्तसङ्घर्ष—having provocation given to him. एधित—ankindled, gen-erated. नरेन्द्रसार्थ—a number of magicians. तन्निराकरिष्यन्—wishing to expel that. शङ्करनृत्तदेश—the place where Śamkara dances, i. e. a cemetery. जरत्साल—i, e. an old sarja tree. कन्याचीरसञ्चय—a collection of rags and patched gar- ments. अतिसंहित—deceived. आकृष्ट—obtained. चेल—a garment. सलिलराशि—the sea. किञ्जल्क॰—variegated with bits of filaments. ०शेखरस्य—decked with the lines of.

 P. 179. अभिसन्धानदक्ष—clever in deceiving or taking advantage of the credulity of. सरहस्य—with their mystical teachings. सषडङ्गानि—for the six añgas see supra, note on षडङ्ग p. 21; it will be seen from that passage that the Kumáras had learnt the Vedas with their añgas. एतत्सङ्ग्रहेण—by his espousal or adoption of. आतङ्क—disease, affliction. सेकैर्निष्कलङ्क॰—whose heads are washed with, i. e. hallowed by. यन्त्र—mystical or astronomical diagrams used as amulets. ग्रहाः—evil spirits supposed to seize upon children &c. कनकलेखाधिष्ठान॰—कनकलेखा अधिष्ठानं यस्य. धनदाज्ञाकर—a Yaksha, क्षत्रियस्य—i̟, e of Jayasimha. अधिगतक्षणः—finding an opportunity or time of leisure, सङ्गृह्णन्—securing in his interest, winning over to his side. ध्यानधीरः—patiently remaining in abstract meditation. स्थानदर्शित॰—showing my knowledge of (lit. possession of) occult things at the proper time. निचाय्य—having found out or ascertained (what was in his mind).

 P. 180. ०लक्षणैकराशेः—the asylum of all auspicious marks. रशना—waist-zone. राजित—decorated लीलाञ्चित॰—with her dark lotus-like eyes sportively bent, or graceful with their sportive movements. दर्शन is used for नेत्र for alliteration with दर्शन. सहते—allows, bears. सह्यतां— wait for; for this sense cf. द्वित्राण्यहान्यर्हसि सोढुमर्हन् Ragh. V. 25. निर्निशाकरार्चिषि—void of moon-light, i. e. on a dark night; or, when the moon-light had passed away. नीरन्ध्र—not broken through, i.e. dense or thick. निगीर्ण—swallowed up, shrouded in. निगाडन—sealed, जलतल॰—to be entered by diving under the surface of water. नीरन्ध्रं—having no opening towards water, छिद्रीकृतान्तरालं—excavated from within. एकतः—in one part (as the Bhûshaṇá has it). The sense of 'near' or 'close to assigned to एकतः by the com-mentator is far-fetched Besides, if सरस्तटं be taken as governed by एकतः, no object is left to अकार्षम्. तीर्थसन्निकृष्टं—close to the landing-stairs. घन—compactly arranged. छत्रच्छिद्राननं—with the opening of the cavity (r the pit he dug out reaching the bottom of the lake) covered with-निर्णिक्त—well washed; pp. of निज् with निर् 3 P. A सन्तान—a belt or multitude of stars. अग्रग्रथित—i. e. the big gem in the middle. The belt of stars is here compared to a necklace of pearls, and the sun to its central gem. सन्तान-हारिहार॰ v. l.—seems to be better; fastened at the end of the necklace in the form of the streaming morning light eclipsing the stars.’ क्षणदा—Lit. 'the giver of rest;' hence, night. गन्धहस्तिन्—गन्धप्रधानो हस्ती an elephant of the best class, the strong smell of whose ichor is unbearable to other elephants and makes them turn tail at his sight. See com. Cf. Ragh. V. 47, VI. 7. कनकशैल—The mountain Meru. The peak of the mountain Meru is compared to a stage and the rising sun to an actor exhibiting the play of light (the gleaming solar rays being reflected back from the gems on the mountain).

 P. 181. घनतरङ्ग—The clouds are the rolling waves in the ocean of the sky. कार्याकार्य—The sun is one of those who keep watch over a man's actions. Cf. आदित्यचन्द्रावनिलोऽनलश्च &c. सहस्राक्षादिक्—the east. रागायित—serving as a red dye or paint; p. p. p. of the denom. from . गैरिकतट—the skirt covered with red chalk; गिरौ भवः गैरिकः, fr. गिरि + इक (ठन् ) साधारणच्छाय—with its appearance like, i. e. appearing like. कदर्थनया—with a desire to harass or torment. अन्तरिक्षाख्येन—आकाश or the sky is considered as one of the eight forms of Śiva; of. श्रुतिविषयगुणा या &c Śák. I. 1. सन्ध्याङ्गनायाः—Hero सन्ध्या is represented as a rival wife. Párvati would be pained to see the body of her lord in another form half-shared by another woman, as indicated by एकस्तन. दिनाधिनाये—i. e. when it was about sunset.जनस्यास्य—is used to avoid the use of माम्, धरणिन्यस्त—i.e. he bowed to his very feet. आदिष्टश्च—he was addressed by me निरीह— unambitious, not aspiring. Cf. साहसे श्रीः प्रतिवसति; उद्योगिनं पुरुषसिंहमुपैति लक्ष्मीः; and निवसन्ति पराक्रमाश्रया न विषादेन समं समृद्धयः। Kir. II. 15. अनाकलित—free from. अर्चितेन—hold in high esteem, honoured. अत्यादरनिश्चितेन—filled with great respect; qualifies जनेन. सततगती॰—the vital airs, i. e. प्राण and others. This is used to avoid the word प्राण. स्खलित- 'rolled back from;' to be construed with जल.स्थागित—covered. As the water rolled over the lotus-plants, it shook their stalks, and frightened the swans as their bodies Were pricked by the points of their thorns. Dissolve—तटात्स्खलितं यज्जलं तेन स्थगितं यज्जलजखण्डं तस्य चलितदण्डानां ये कण्टकास्तेषामग्रैर्दलिता देवा येषां ते; now see Bhûshanâ quoted in the com, त्रासजर्जर—broken through fear.

 P. 182.निचाय्य—having definitely known or observed; of. supra, p. 179. स्थिरतर॰—firmly fixed or fastened by the chains of deep love. असहनीय &c.—She will not brook any delay in obtaining your sight, i. e. she will be longing for your company. नात्यादर॰—without much concern, easily. धीर—oultured, developed आकलय्य—having considered the matter जालिकशतं च &c.—This precaution was necessary to see that there were no crocodiles &c. there; cf. Ragh. आनायिभिस्तां (=सरयूं) अपकृष्टनक्रां XVI. 65. अन्तरङ्गनर—people in whom you can confide. त्रिंशद्वण्डा— for a space of thirty daṇḍas (a measure of length equal to 4 hastas, or, according to the Oom., 16 hastas). रन्ध्रदर्शनासहैः—who could not see any mischief in it. तथास्थितः &c.—being ready to carry out my instructions and firmly resolved to secure the maiden.जनान्त―region, a secluded place. अनाचर्य—without having accomplished. यथार्ह—as may be agreeable to you.

 P.183. धरणितलतैतिलगण—The gods on earth, i, e. the Brâhmaņas. वर्तिका—a wick, a piece of cloth wrapped round the end of a stick (and used as a torch). ग्रस्त—swallowed up, dispelled. निःसङ्गता—indifference, the state of not being attached to the world. निरागस—innocent. जालिक—fishermen, men with nets. संहतकर्णनासं—with the holes of his ears and nostrils stopped. हास्तिनं—the depth of which was an elephant's stature. The constr. is हास्तिनं सरस्तलं गतं तं नकलीलया नीरातिनिलीनयायी अहं &o Some read गतः for गसं; in this case the sentence will close at हास्तिनं; he dived into the water and reached the bottom of the lake the depth of which was an elephant's height.' नक्रलीलया—with the ease ( and caution) of a crocodile. अतिनिलीन॰—&c.-moving forth, having dived deep into water. कालदण्डपट्टना॰—tremendous (killing) like the strokes of the rod of Yama. निर्दयद्त्तनिग्रहः—mercilessly pommelled. This incident of the story, viz. the supposed transformation of the person of the king into a superior form, is a repetition of Upahárvarman's personating himself as Vikatavarman after he had murdered him in his narration of his account. Perhaps in writing out this part of the story Daṇḍin got his inspiration from the story of Taruṇachandra, a physician, related in the Brihatkathá The physician, having pro-mised to cure विनयशील, king of Vilâsapura, of the deformity brought on by old age, in eight months, asked him to dwell in an underground cellar. But, as the king under his treatment instead of becoming young wasted away, the physician secretly murdered him, threw his body into a well with its mouth closed, and having introduced a young man of similar features into the cave, kept him there for some days. He afterwards brought him out and presented him before the subjects as the king brought back to youth again by his medication. Cf. अभूच्छ्रीकण्ठनिलये विलासपुरनामनि। पुरे विनयशालाख्यो नाम्नाऽन्वर्थेन भूपतिः ॥.......इत्यादि चिन्तयन्सोऽथ सदस्याहूय भूपतिः। वैद्यं तरुणचन्द्राख्यं निजगाद कृतादरः॥ अप्यस्ति काचिद्युक्तिः सा ययेयं वार्यते जरा।...... एकस्त्वं भूगृहे मासानष्टो यदिदमौषधम्। उपयुङ्क्षे ततो देव जरामपनयामि ते॥...... आजहार कमप्येकं पुरुषं तादृशाकृतिम्। राजानं त्वां करोमीति युवानं कृतसंविदम्॥ ततः सुरङ्गां भूगेहे दूराद्दत्वाऽथ तं नृपम्। सुप्तं हत्वा तया नीत्वा सोऽन्धकूपेऽक्षिपन्निशि॥ तयैव पुरुषं तं च तरुणं तत्र भूगृहे। प्रवेश्य स्थापयामास सुरङ्गां पिदधे च ताम्॥ ततः स सर्वाः प्रकृतीर्वैद्योऽन्येद्युरभाषत। अजरोऽयं कृतस्तावत्षङ्भिर्मासैर्मया नृपः &c. Kathàs. VII. 6. 60–64.

 P. 184. अत्यचित्रीयत—seemed a great wonder. See supra, note on चित्रीया p. 151. चण्डतरदण्डी॰—the very fierce staff-bearers. रसनयन॰—with my eyes stripped of the pleasure of sleep owing to the joy (रस आनन्दजनितभावविशेषः) I felt then. नयन—getting; stands for, प्राप्ति which could not be used. नयनरस॰ v. 7.—in this case the comp. becomes clumsy; we have to take it as नयनाभ्यां रसेन निरस्ता &c. दिग्गजशिरः—the frontal globe &c.यथासदृशा॰—who waited upon me with the proper formalities. अजेय—not to be controlled or overpowered by (to be construed with इन्द्रियाणां). नीरजसा—free from all foulness. सरसिज &c.—सरसिजदलेन सदृशी ०दलसन्निकाशा छाया यस्य; the complexion of which resembles that of lotus-petals,नि—or नी–काश expresses resemblance when at the end of a comp.; see Amara. II. 10. 38, नरकशासन—the killer of Naraka (a demon, king of Pràgjyo-tisha), Krishna; hence, Vishņu. सरसिजासना॰—This shows that Brahmâ-worship was in vogue in Daṇḍin's time. क्लेश निरसन—sufficient to dissipate their troubles. सातिशयं—with supreme power. आदिराज—may be Manu; see, however, note on the same at p. 146, last line.

 P. 185. हर्षकाष्ठा—The highest pitch of joy. लासयन्ती—causing to dance or glitter ललिताञ्चित—gracefully bent. दन्तच्छदकीसलया—her sprout-like (red and tender) lip. हर्षजल॰—Her eyes, void of collyrium, were filled with the moisture of the tears of joy. जालं—a trick meant to entrap. स्नेहनिर्यन्त्रणं—freely through affection. सहचर्याः—his beloved Kanakalekhâ. निरर्गलीकृता॰—निर्गता अर्गला यस्य तथा कृतः (set at liberty) च अतिसत्कृतश्च &c.

 P. 186. अतिकष्टं तपः—This is said to please him. प्रज्ञा सत्वयोः—ingenuity, and courage or mental calibre.

 क्लिश्यन्—suffering from. क्लिष्ट—in distress. उदञ्चन्—drawing up निष्कल—an old man; this word has occurred before on p. 171. वंशनाली॰—drawn up in the hollow piece of a long bamboo. पाषाण॰—knocked down with stones.

 P. 187. विदर्भोनाम &c.—The great kingdom of Vidarbha extended from the banks of the Krishná to near the banks of the Narmadá. On account of its great size it was also called Maháráshtra. Its capital, Kuṇḍinapura, is probably the modern Beder. According to the Nalachampú, Kuṇḍinapura was situated on the river Varadá (=Wardhá). The famous race of the Bhojas was a branch of the Yádavas. अंशावतारः—A righteous king is often described as a partial manifestation of Dharma; of अपवर्गमहोदयार्थयोर्भुवमंशाविव धर्मयोर्गतौ। Ragh. VIII. 16. अतिसत्व—of extraordinary prowess or courage. Mark the author's command of language, in how many ways he expresses the same idea. उदम—distinguished or ennobled by. उत्थानशील—ambitious of greatness. शास्त्रप्रमाणः—following the authority of, guided by, the Śάstras; defender of the faith. शक्यभव्य॰—an undertaker of things possible to accomplish, of public utility and benefi- cial in the end. See com. Daṇḍin seems to have taken this phraseology from Kauțilîya, as remarked by Mr. Agashe; cf. शक्यकल्यभव्यारम्भिस्थिरकर्मानुरक्तप्रकृतिभ्यो विशेषः। शक्यारम्भी विषह्यं कर्मारभेत। कल्यारम्भी निर्दोषम्। भव्यारम्भी कल्याणोदयम्। Kau Nît. Adhi. VII., Oh. 8, 114, 115. प्रभावयिता—who advanced or promoted. उद्भावयिता—raising to dignity. अवितृष्णः—never apathetic, ever eager for. नदीष्ण—proficient in. नेदिष्ठ—lit. nearest to; hence, conversant or well acquainted with धर्मार्थसंहिता—sacred lore and political science. सुकृत—favour obligation.प्रत्यवेक्षितृ —A superintendent of, who himself carefully looked after. सर्वाध्यक्ष—superintendents of different de partments. कृतकर्मणः—those rendering signal services. Cf. क्रियापवर्गेष्वनुजीविसात्कृताः कृतज्ञतामस्य वदन्ति सम्पदः। Kir. I. 14.

 P. 188. दैवमानुषीणामा॰—The calamities coming from deities are such as fire, water, disease, famine, death, &e. ; and from men are such as (corrupt) royal officers, thieves, enemies, favourites of the king, and the greed of the king himself. हुताशनो जलं व्याधिर्दुर्भिक्षं मरणं तथा। इति पञ्चविधं दैवं व्यसनं भानुषं ततः॥ आयुक्तकेभ्यश्वौरेभ्यः परेभ्यो राजवल्लभात्। पृथिवीपतिलोभाश्च नराणां पञ्चधा मतम् (i. e.भयम्)॥ Kâm. N. XIII. 20; V. 82. See Ragh. I. 60. षाङ्गुण्य—The six ex-pedients to be employed in foreign politics; these are (see com.)—(1) सन्धि or peace or alliance ; ( 2 ) विग्रह or making of war (when other remedies fail); (3) यान or marching against the enemy when one is in a position to do so and one's own king. dom is well protected; (4) आसन or halting (waiting for oppor-tunity &c.); (5) द्वैध or द्वैधीभाव duplicity, sowing seeds of dis-cord among the ranks of the enemy; and (6) आश्रम or seeking shelter (the help of a powerful king &c.). मनुमार्गेण—According to the line of conduct chalked out by Manu. Here Manu may refer to the great law-giver or to the king of that name. Cf.Ragh. I. 17; of also Yájñavalkya—कुलानि जातीः श्रेणीश्व गणाञ्जानपदानपि। स्वधर्माच्चलितान्राजा विनीय स्थापयेत्पथि॥ पुण्यश्लोकः—one whose name it is auspicious to repeat; one of holy fame.-पुरुषायुषं—the full period of human life, one hundred years. A final अ is added to आयुस् when preceded by पुरुष &c, by अचतुर विचतुर..निःश्रेयसपुरुषायुष द्वयायुषत्र्यायुष....। Pàṇ. V. 4. 77.

 तदायति—who owed his prosperity or power to him (his father); i. e. who succeeded to his prosperous kingdom. Or it may mean “equal to him in prowesŚ (तस्य इव आयतिर्यस्य ).दैवात्—as fate would have it. दण्डनीति—the science of poli-tios. दण्ड—government; fr. दम् 4 P. to control, to subdue ; दभ्यते अनेन, ड being added by ज् मन्ताद्दु। Unádi. I 114. It is thus praised by Kámandaka—एकैव दण्डनीतिस्तु विद्येत्यौशनसा स्थिताः। तस्यां तु सर्वविद्यानामारम्भाः समुदाह्रताः॥ II. 5. नत्यादृतः—did not much devote himself to, did not at all take interest in. प्रगस्भवाक्—eloquent in speech; whose words could make an im-pression. The following is a sound piece of advice which can be given only by a consummate politician and a veteran states-man. आत्ससम्पत्—qualities of the soul, personal accomplish-ments. These are शास्त्रं, प्रज्ञा &c.; see the quotation from Kám. (line 7 of the Com.) विस्तरेषु—Mark the derivation; ‘with refer- ence to speech or words; the word is विस्तर; स च शब्दस्य विस्तरः। Amara. it is formed by adding अ (अम्) to स्तृृ; the affix घञ् (अ ) is added to it with the prep. षि (which gives विस्तार) in the sense of extension or magnitude. We have a third word विष्टर from the same root in the sense of 'a seat', by वृक्षासनयोर्विष्टरः। Páṇ. VIII. 3. 93. विस्तार—development, culture; see above, on विस्तर. आत्मसं॰—refining, polishing, necessary for it. Cf. चतसृष्वपि ते विवेकिनी नृप विद्यासु निरूढिमागता (मतिः)। &c. Kir. II. 6; and आत्मा प्रयत्नादर्थेम्यो मनः समधितिष्ठति। संयोगादात्ममनसोः प्रवृत्तिरुपजायते॥ विज्ञानं हृदयं चित्तं मनो बुद्धिश्च सत्समम्। अनेनात्मा करोतीह प्रवर्तन निवर्तने॥ प्रयत्नज्ञानसंस्कारा आत्मलिङ्गमुदाहृतम्॥ Kám.-Nît. I. 28, 30, 31.अर्थशास्त्रेषु—see supra, p. 147. अग्निसंशोधिता—the simile shows that proficiency in the science of government is of vital importance to a king. बुद्धिशून्यः—Here बुध्दि means diplomatic talent, statesmanship. 

 P.188. अत्युच्छ्रितः—very mighty, supreme. अध्यारुह्यमाणं—surpassed in diplomacy; brought under their power. विभज्य—assigning their proper province to them.अयथावृत्त—not acting with a right perception of objects to be effected and their means. कर्मसु प्रतिहन्यमानः—defeated or thwarted in his objects (undertakings ). योगक्षेम—the acquisition of things not obtained and their preservation. See com. अतिक्रान्त॰—when contempt is shown to authority. सर्वा स्थितीः &c.—will upset all order. निर्मर्यादः—when people transgress the bounds of duty, ie, are demoralized.आगमदीप &c—आगम means here नीतीशास्त्र or the science of the conduct of human affairs. लोकयात्रा—the conduct of worldly affairs, the course of worldly life. व्यवहित—hidden from view, secret; or, impeded by obstacles.विप्रकृष्ट—beyond the reach or ken of senses. तेन हीनः &c.—Cf. अनेकसंशयोच्छेदि परोक्षार्थस्य दर्शकम्। सर्वस्य लोचनं शास्त्रं यस्य नास्त्यन्ध एव सः॥ अप्रतिहतवृत्ति— tho operation of which is never obstructed. अर्थदर्शन—discern ment of political and other ends or objects. आगमय—obtain an insight into, gain proficiency in. तदर्थानु॰—by carrying out or acting up to its precepts. आवर्जितशक्ति॰—having at command the three regal powers. For the Śaktis, see infra, p. 208.
