 P. 190.प्रसादवित्तः—who enjoyed special royal favour; lit. who was known to be a royal favourite. याद्य—is of four kinds, viz, ततं वीणादिकं वाद्यं (such as playing on lutes, violins and other stringed instuments) आनध्दं मुरजा (tabors &o.)—विक्रम। वंशादिकं (playing on harps and other wind instruments) तु सुषिरं काम्यतालादिकं (beating of cymbals and other instruments struck together in pairs) घनम्॥ Amar. I. 7. 4. अबाह्य—not out, i.e. well-acquainted with. अयन्त्रितमुखः—garrulous, of licentious tongue. बहुभङ्गि॰—clever in periphrastical and enigmatical speeches. परमर्मा॰—habitually seeking another's weak or vulnerable points. परिवाद॰—delighting in circulating scandal. पैशुन्यपण्डितः—adopt.in craft. उत्कोच—bribes. सकल॰—a professor of all wicked acts, of every kind of inequity. कामतन्त्र॰—a pilot of libertinism or licentiousness. कामतन्त्र—the science or ways of sexual enjoyment. कुमारसेवकः—one who had served the king from the time he was a boy or crown prince. "The arguments which are urged by one of his (Anantavarman's) favourites," remarks Prof. Wilson, " in be half of idle pleasures and in detraction of grave advisers and ministers, while they speak the language of the profligate in all ages, afford some curious insight into the abuses of official authority which prevailed in Indian Government at the time of the composition.” With the introductory part of this admonition may be compared Nai.-Ch. XVII. Ślokas 37-96, as pointed by Mr. Agashe.उच्चावचेः—of all sorts ; lit. high and low; उदक् च अवाक् च उच्चावचम्; irregularly formed. See supra, p.कदर्थयन्तः—teasing, harassing. प्रेत्य—after death. Cf. मृतः स्मरति जन्मानि मृते कर्मफलोर्मयः। अन्यमुक्तैर्मृते तृप्तिरित्यलं धूर्तवार्तया॥ Nai. XVII. 53. अभ्युदया॰—fortune or prosperity of the highest kind or exalted nature मुण्डयित्वा etc.—This refers to the initiatory rite at the Agnish" toma sacrifice, in which the head of the Yajamána is first shaven, then tied with the kuŚa grass &c. as stated here.पाषण्डिनः—religious hypocrites, heretics. This refers to the Jaina Bhikshus who advocate renunciation of worldly ties, such as wife, sons, &c. पटुजातीय—a clever or shrewd follow. अस्यै मृगतृष्णिकायै—in pursuit of this mirage. हस्तगतं—what he has in his possession. ककिणि—an inferior coin equal to twenty cowries. कार्षापण—a copper coin of a particular value. See Bhúshaṇa.

 P. 191. चतस्त्रः राज॰—See Kám. N. II. 2.त्रयी—the three Vedas, वार्ता—practical arts such as agriculture, commerce &c. The Devî-Purâṇa thus accounts for the name—पश्वादिपालनाद्देवि कृषिकर्मान्तकारणात्। वर्तनादारणणादाऽपि वार्ता सा एवं गीयते॥ आन्वीक्षिकी—logic and metaphysics. दण्डनीति—The science of government. Cf. Kâm -Nît. II. quoted in the com., and एकैव दण्डनीतिस्तु विद्येत्यौशनसाः स्थिताः। तस्यां तु सर्वविद्यानामारम्भाः सम्प्रतिष्ठिताः॥ Kám. Nît. II. 5. विष्णुगुप्त—better known as Châṇakyà, a celebrated statesman and diplomatist who flourished in the latter part of the 4th century B. C. He is the author of a work on political science and state-craft called Oháṇakyanîti after him. मौर्यार्थे—Maurya is Chandragupta, king of Pàtaliputra, and the hero of the Mudrârákshasa, According to Indian authorities, Châṇakya was his principal adviser and minister, he having got the throne of the Nandas chiefly through his help. शास्त्रान्तरानुबन्धि—is connected with other Śàstras (and depends upon them for its thorough understanding). Here begins a satirical description of the study and effects of Dandanîti or political science. पुत्र दारमपि &c.—For the Nitî- sástra lays down stringent rules for the daily course of action of a king. He is not to trust too much his queen or sons, must eat a measured quantity of food and of a particular kind, well examined by experts and so on. See Kâm.-Nît. VII. आत्मकुक्षेः &c.—for their own belly, i.e. meals.

 उत्थितेन &c.—Now the author tells us, closely following the authority of Cháṇakya, how the regal day of 24 hours was apportioned into eight parts, particular duties being assign-ed to each. मुष्टि—has a technical meaning, according to the Bhûshana (which see); for a different sense see supra, P. 121 (मुष्टिवधः) ; here मुष्टि means a measure used in checking the account of the income from and expenditure on account of the country, while अर्धमुष्टि is to be used in connexion with a village. See also the Laghudîpikâ. अभ्यन्तरीकृत्य—having admitted for reckoning; or taking into account. It may here mean ' having eaten a morsel' also.आयव्ययजातं—all the receipts and expenditure. आहरणोपायान्—modes or ways of defrauding. Cf तेषां हरणोपायाश्चत्वारिंशत्। Ohân. II. 8. विकल्पयितारः—adopt as alternatives; hence, multiply into. The precepts are द्वितीये पौरजानपदानां कार्याणि पश्येत्। तृतीये स्नानभोजनं सैवेत स्वाध्यायं च कुर्वीत। चतुर्थे हिरण्यम्प्रतिग्रहमध्यक्षांश्च कुर्वीत। पञ्चमे मन्त्रिपरिषदं पत्रसम्प्रेषणेन मन्त्रयेत। चारगुप्तबोधनानि च बुध्येत। I. 19. जयपराजयौ—decision in favour of or otherwise. For प्राड्विवाक see p. 131, last line. अर्थैः—by securing their own objects or consulting their own advantage.पापने…………..योजयन्ति—Cf. ईदृशे व्यवहाराग्नौ मन्त्रिभिः परिपातिताः। स्थाने खलु महीपाला गच्छन्ति कृपणां दृशाम्॥ Mrish. IX. 41; see also the next ál. यावदन्धःपरिणामः— until the food eaten is digested. विषभयं—A king runs the danger of having his food poisoned. Cf. याने शय्यासने पाने भोज्ये वस्त्रे विभूषणे। सर्वत्रैवाप्रमत्तः स्याद्वर्जेत विषदूषितम्॥ Kám. N. VI. 9 (and the succeeding ślokas wherein several modes for detecting poison are given).

 P. 192. हिरण्य॰—The king is to receive and make presents in the fourth division of the day. प्रसारयत्रेव—i .e. he has scarcely time to do that. मन्त्रचिन्ता—deliberation or consideration of political schemes with the ministers and coun-cillors. मध्यस्था इव—outwardly appearing to be neutral (in-different and impartial). Or, after the Bhúshaṇá, like pleaders.,मिथः सम्भूय—forming a league with one another. दोषगुणौ—merits and defects (of a political scheme). दूताः—ambas-sadors, envoys. स्वेच्छया—according to their will (as would suit their purpose). विपरिवर्तयन्तः—perverting, mis representing and confounding. उपजीवन्ति—live upon, i. e. they enrich themselves by getting money and the other objects they seek from the members of their own party, the party of enemy and of the allies or friends. बाह्या॰—see supra, p. 134. प्रशमयन्त इव—They do not really want to suppress insurrections, but want war; of. मन्त्रिणः स्वार्थतात्पर्याद्दीर्घमिच्छन्ति विग्रहम्। तेषां च भोग्यतामेति दीर्घकार्याकुलो नृपः॥ Kám. N. XII. 41 अवशमगृह्णन्ति—hold him helpless in their power. सोऽस्यैता॰—स दह्यतां स्वैर v. l. is not so good, as the speaker nowhere condemns a thing directly, but leaves it to be so understood from his words which have an ironical force. तिस्त्रस्त्रि॰—Náḍikás, i. e, ghatikás (a घटिका = 24 minutes), or an hour and a half. What a long interval of time ! चतुरङ्ग॰—In the Kaut. we have हस्त्यश्वरथायुधीयान्पश्येत्. प्रत्यवेक्षण—reviewing of. सेनापति॰— सेनापतेः सखा सेनापतिसखः तस्य; the comp. must be so dissolved, and not as а Bah. विक्रमचिन्ता—projects of victory, i. e. how to make the army efficient. गूढपुरुषाः—men in disguise, secret emissaries. In the first portion of the night the king is to receive the reports of his spies and envoys. शस्त्राग्नि &c.—Persons appointed to employ weapons or fire or to administer poison (to compass particular ends). These were the ways of getting an unwelcome person or enemy out of the way. Chânakya himself takes precautions to thwart the efforts of Rákshasa in this direction, in the Mudrárâkshasa. अनुष्ठेय—to be set to work or appointed. तूर्यघोषेण—seated, enjoys rest, listening to the play of trumpets; or तृतीये may be taken with the preceding sentence; thus द्वितीये... आरभते तृतीये; ‘in the second watch he takes his food, and attends to his daily course of studies in the third. 'The next sentence would then begin with तूर्यघोषेण &c.—going to bed on hearing the sound of the trumpet, he enjoys sleep &c. किल—as they say, i. e. he is not likely to get sleep. अजस्र—constant; fr. जस् 4 Conj. P. with ₹ added by नमिकम्पिस्म्यजसकमहिंसदीपो रः । Pán. III. 2. 167; जसिः (ie the root जस्) नञ्पूर्वः क्रियासातत्ये वर्तते। नम्रः, कम्प्रः, स्मेरः, अजस्रः &c. ०मनसः—with his mind labouring under the torture of; or, tor- mented by being on the stretch of constant anxiety. मन्त्रग्रहः—listening to the counsels of the ministers; सप्तमे मन्त्रमध्यासीत गूढपुरुषांश्च प्रेषयेत्। Kaut N. नाम—as is well-known. वीतशुल्कबाध—free from the fear of being subject to taxes. वणिज्यया—by disposing of or selling वणिजः कर्म वणिज्या formed by affixing य by दूतवणिग्भ्यां च quoted in the Kásikâ. This is not found in the Bháshya, says the Sid.-Kan.—माधवस्तु वणिज्याशब्दः स्वभावात्स्त्रीलिङ्गः भावे एव प्रत्ययः न तु कर्मणि इत्याह। भाष्ये तु दूतवणिग्भ्यां च इति नात्स्येव। So this should be dissolved as वणिजे साधुः, by affixing यत् (य) by तत्र साधुः। Pán. IV. 4. 98. The word वाणिज्यं is formed by affixing व्यञ् (य) by Pán. V. 1. 124. लेशेन—lit in some part; making some occasion for it. क्लेशेन v.l.—with difficulty, taking pains to create an occasion.

 P. 193. The eighth portion is appropriated to the priest and religious ceremonies, after which the business of the day is resumed. गुणवत्—prolifits of fruit, efficacious.ब्रह्मकल्पाः—almost equal to Brahman. This is ironical. स्वस्त्ययन—स्वस्ति ईयते अनेन a rite averting evil and leading to a blissful end. Mr. Agashe quotes the following from the Śabdakalpadruma—माङ्गल्यकर्मारम्भकालविवक्ष्यमाणमन्त्रोच्चारणपूर्वकतण्डुलविकिरणम्। तन्मन्त्रो यथा—अमुककर्मणि पुण्याहं भवतां ब्रुवन्तु। इत्युक्ते पुण्याहमिति ब्राह्मणैस्त्रिरुक्त अमुककर्मणि ऋद्धिं भवन्तो ब्रुवन्तु। तत ऋभ्यतामिति ब्राह्मणास्त्रिर्बुयुः। ततः स्वस्ति न इन्द्रो वृद्धश्रवाः स्वस्ति नः पूषा विश्ववेदाः। स्वस्ति नस्तार्क्ष्योऽरिष्टनमिः स्वस्ति नो बृहस्पतिर्दध तु। इति पठित्वा तण्डुलान् विकिरेत्।.अद्याप्यप्राप्त॰—wbo have not as yet received any donations. स्वर्ग्य—loading to heaven, conferring heavenly happiness. आयुष्यं—conferring long life; आयुः प्रयोजनमस्य; fr. आयुस् + य (त्). तन्मुखेन—through their medium अविहित॰—This and the following adj. compounds qualify अहर्निशं used as a noun. अविरल॰—full of torments. नयतः—passing. स्वमण्डल—his own territory. आविश्वासः—mistrust. People see a motive in all his acts as they don't seem to be natural, and so do not trust him. यावता च &c—To what extent the course of worldly existence can proceed without policy, is seen from the experience of ordinary life; i. e. it is quite clear from our experience of daily life that the course of the world can go on unimpeded without policy. Some read नं याति लोक॰; this means—to what extent does not proceed without &c.; i. e. worldly experience itself teaches what policy to follow in this world. नात्र शास्त्रेणार्थः—We need have no recourse to Śa'stra in this matter.अति यन्त्र णा—too great or absurd restraint. सामादिरुपाय॰—see com. मन्त्रिचक्र—hypocritical or cunning counsellors; a
Tatp. Comp. of the Nitya class; see मन्त्रकर्कश—advocates of a stern enforcement of counsel or policy. शुक्र—Śukrácharya, the preceptor of the demons and the author of a Smriti, named after him.आङ्गिरस—a great sage, one of the inspired law-givere (an author of one of the chief Smritis) and a writer on astronomy, विशालाक्ष—name of Śiva, as the author of a śastra बाहुदन्तिपुत्रः—Son of Indra (बाहुदन्तिन्), name of Jayadatta, author of a Tantra. The two are referred to as writers on Nîti by Kautilya; see Prak. IV. p. 13, Mysore Ed. पराशर is well known; his Smriti is considered as the chief authority in the Kali age.
  

 P. 194. पठन्तश्च &c.—Those who are well-read are taken in by those who have never read Śástra. ननुइदमुपपन्न &c.—Does not Your Majesty possess all this, viz. सर्वलोकस्य &c. अयातयाम—यातयाम (see com.) means the time for the en- joyment of which is gone;' exhausted, stale; hence अया॰—what is fresh, youthful. बहुमार्गवि॰—On account of its pointing out diverse ways and therefore not leading to one definite end. अमुक्तसंशयेन—never giving up its misgivings (i.e. not arriving at a definite conclusion as. to the adoption of one way). तन्त्रावाप—giving attention to internal as well as to foreign matters (see com ); cares of state-matters. पादातं—fr. पदाति + अण् (अ) by Páp. IV. 2. 38; see com. पादाभ्यामतात इति पदातिः अन्यार्जित—another's property. अल्पाल्पं—very small. वयःखण्डम्—a portion of life.अर्जयन्त एव &c.—perish while they are acquir ing money; i s. they never curb their desires. आस्वादयितुं—Inf of the cau. of स्वद् 1 A ; or read आस्वादितुम् अन्तरङ्गेषु—men enjoying your confidence, trusted men. अप्सरःप्रति॰—counterparts nymphs of heaven in beauty. पनगोष्ठी—drinking parties. यथार्हे—यो यो अर्हः यथार्हः suited to i. e. capable of enjoying, every object; cf तं तमाचरतानन्दं स्वच्छन्दं यं यमिच्छथ। Nai. XVII, 50. पज्जाङ्ग—see com. अञ्जलिचुम्बितचूडः—with his folded hands closely placed across his brow क्रिडा॰—क्रीडानिर्भरमतिः v. l. his mind deeply set on spor

 P. 195. एषु दिनेषु—In the days that followed प्रस्तुतेऽर्थे—the business in hand or for consideration. अचित्तज्ञ इति—Because he did not correctly read his (the king's) thoughts.अरु चितेऽर्थे—In matters distasteful or disagreeable to him. अक्षिगतः—a thorn in the eye; an eye-sore; an object of hatred. विलोभनवस्तुनि—choice gifts, lit. things that are tempting or attractive. प्रत्यवेक्षते—has regard for, cares for. आसन्नकार्येषु—things that immediately concern him; his private matters. अवष्टभ्यमान—encroached upon, occupied by. मत्समान॰—the faults of those holding equal rank with me; or मत्समानदोषान् may mean 'whose faults are similar to mine,' i. e. who have the courage to advise him like me. विगर्हयति—'censures more par- ticularly (वि). मर्मणि &c.—so as to touch my vitals, so as to cause intense pain. प्रतिक्षिपति—disapproves, rejects. स्खलित—blunders. मूर्खौः &c.—which is an insult, to them. सत्यमाह चा॰—the reference is to Ohánakya's अनर्थ्याश्च प्रिया दृष्ट्वा चित्तज्ञानानु-वर्तिनः। and अप्रिया अपि दक्षाः स्वस्तद्भावाद्ये बहिष्कृताः। Prak. 92. चित्तज्ञानानुवर्तिनः—following the bents of mind of the king, ministering to the desires of the king. अनर्थ्याः—undesirable, i. e. wicked or bad counsellors. अनर्था : v. l.—अविद्यमानोऽर्थो येभ्यः from whom no object can be gained; hence, bad. तद्भाव बहिष्कृताः—out of, i.e. not knowing, his intentions. पितृपैतामहैः—servants of his father and grand- father.अश्रूयमाणवाचः—when our counsels are not listened to. अश्मक—An old name of Travancore. The Aámakas were also inhabitants of Northern India according to Varáha- mihira (Brih. Sam. XIV. 34) who also speaks of them along with the Andhras (XVI. 11). Cf. "A tribe of Aśmakas, or Assakas, was settled, at the time when the Sutta-Nipâta was composed, on the banks of the Godávarî. This is, no doubt, the same tribe whose name appears in inscriptions at Ajanta of about the sixth century A. D., and they therefore probably occupied land near the sources of this river in the Western Deccan, south of Berar." (Mark Collins ).

 प्रकृतिस्थमापादयेयुः—will restore him to his natural disposition, reclaim him. अनर्थेषु—undesirable pursuits. सुलभव्यलीक—easily leading to or bringing on misery or pain. द्वेष— disgust for स्तम्भितपिशुनजिह्वः— putting a curb over my tongue, and so not allowing it to move in giving advice, refraining from counselling. Or it may mean— ‘putting (or exercising) a check on the tongues of wicked (or, designing) men.'

 **P. 196**. एवं गते—When the minister had taken this resolve and held himself aloof from state-affairs. असद्वृत्तः &c.—being avowedly expelled by his father for his licentious conduct. Mark the meaning of नाम 'under the pretence of,' 'giving out that;’ *cf. supra*, कार्तान्तिको नाम भूत्वा p. 159. शिल्पकारिणी—a dancing girl. छत्र—in disguise (numerous servants in different disguises). विहारभद्र—the king's favourite; see *supra*, p. 190.सङ्क्रम—course of conduct; or, by this 'channel.' राजन्यास्पदमलभत—obtained a hold upon the king. लब्धरन्भ्र—Lit. when he found a loop-hole for him to enter, i.e. got into the king's confidence or getting an opportunity—refers to Chandrapálita. स refers to राजन्. औपकारिकी—conferring benefits. The author now points out ten beneficial results of chase. व्यायामोत्कर्ष—best kind of exercise. जङ्घाजव—fleetness or swiftness of foot आशयग्निदीप्ति—Lit. kindling of the gastric fire; quickening or improving the appetite. आशय—the stomach. मेद—fat. स्थैर्य—firmness, power of endurance; see com. अतिलाघव—great agility. अवस्थान्तरेषु—different states of the mind as affected by fright. चित्तचष्टित—thoughts and actions. हरिण॰......... प्रतिक्रिया—This (preventing injury to crops) was the chief object which the Śástrakâras had in view in allowing kings the right of मृगया. स्थलपथ॰—clearing the roads of the pestilence of tigers and others. Cf. with the ideas expressed here Śâk. II. 4, 5 quoted in the com., and Ragh. **IX. 49.** Daṇḍin must have had these before his mind's eye when he wrote this passage. विविधकर्म॰—fit for undertakings of various kinds. आटविकवर्गविश्रम्भण—gaining the confidence of the people that tenant the wilds. सन्धुक्षण—quickening, stirring up प्रत्यनीकवित्रासन—intimidation of hostile parties. आशय—the mind (generosity of heart). अनवस्थान—Lit. unsettled state; uncertainty, or instability. अविधेयत्व—the state of not being subject to, power to control the feelings of. अमर्ष—highspiritedness (which does not allow one to bear the rise of another or to bear insults tamely ); cf. अमर्षशून्येन जनस्य जन्तुना न जातहार्देन न विद्विषादरः। **Kir. I. 33.**

** P. 197**. **अक्षहस्तभूम्यादेि॰—**With ** **regard to the handling of dice and moving the pieces in the squares &c. **अत्यन्तदुरुप्॰**—too subtle to be marked or noticed. **कूटकर्मणां**—clever tricks. **उपल॰**—by marking or observing them. **एकविषयो॰**—since the whole is focused or concentrated (lit. made to converage to the fulfilment of) one object (viz success in the game). **अव्यवसाय॰—**the concomitant of, i. e. to be gained by, determined perseverance. **प्रतिसंसर्ग—**opposition to. **शरीरयापन—**passing of life. **अर्थधर्मौ**—by the Vârttika **धर्मादिष्वनियमः** the comp. may be धर्मार्थौ or अर्थधर्मौ. **पुष्कल**—praiseworthy, noble. **पुरुषभीमानः**—the pride of one's being a male, i. e. gifted with manly (virile) power (पौरुष). **अलोभक्लिष्ट**—not hampered by greed, as one has to be necessarily generous. **अलब्धोपलब्धि**—for, as such a man has constant need of money, he must try to obtain what he has not yet obtained. The adjectives अलब्ध &c. may also similary refer to females (अलब्धाया उपलब्धिः, लब्धाया अनुरक्षणं &c.). **भुक्तानुसन्धान**—keeping an eye on what is spent (so that there should be no waste); or, **भुक्ताया अनु॰**—keeping wellpleased (a woman) after enjoyment. **शरीरसंस्का॰**—संस्कार means bodily cleanliness and decoration. A man wishing to win the hearts of ladies must pay special attention to his bodily decoration and dress. **सुभग॰**—charming attractive. **सम्भावनीयता**—imposing appearance, impressiveness; lit. capacity to win public esteem. **व्यपेक्षा**—regard or consid eration for, being kind to servants. **उद्रिक्त्तस॰**—great nobility of mind; or, rather, excessive virility or vitality. **स्पृहणियवयो॰**—preservation or keeping up of enviable youth (by the flush wine imparts to the face). **अहङ्कारप्रकर्ष**—extreme sense of self-respect; an overwhelming sense of self (due to the influence of wine). **अङ्गन॰**—its exciting or in flaming passion. **सन्धुक्षणम्**—the stimulation of. **अपराधप्रमार्जन—**blotting out from memory crimes committed **मन शल्य**—anything rankling in the mind. **अशाठ्यशांसन्**—declaring or indicating absence of deceit or guile, i. e. straightforwardless. **अश्राव्य॰ **v. l.—disclosing secrets.
**उपबृंहण**—increasing. **अननुबन्ध**—-break, cessation. **आनन्दैकतानता**—complete absorption in joy; or, the continuons enjoyment of unmixed joy. **संविभाग**—the habit of distributing or allotting shares to others.   

 **P.198**. **अनुत्तराणि**—unsurpassed, excellent beyond comparison. **विलसितानि**—sportings, dalliances. **भयार्तिहरणात्** &c.—It is Is useful in war, since it makes one insensible to fear and pain वाक्यारुष्यं &c.—These are set forth as qualifications necessary for a king. The Bhú. does not seem to be right in connecting these with पान, as the इति after साङ्ग्रामिकत्वं closes the subject. दुषणानि अर्थानां—Lit. misappropriation of money, imposing money-penalty, extravagance, &c.अर्थदूषण is mentioned by writers on polity as one of the faults of kings due to anger; see Ka'm. quoted further on. It is of four kinds—अदानमादानं विनाशः परित्यागश्चार्थस्येत्यर्थदूषणम्। Ka’u. Artha **VIII. 3. 129.** औपकारिकाणि—Here it will be seen that Chandra. speaks approvingly (of course with a sinister motive) of the very things which are condemned by writers on polity as grave faults in a king; cf. वाग्दण्डयोश्च पारुष्यमर्थदूषणमेव च। स्मृतं व्यसनतत्त्वज्ञैः क्रोधजं व्यसनत्रयम्॥ कामजं मृगया द्यूतं स्त्रियः पानं तथैव च। व्यसनं व्यसनार्थज्ञैश्चतुर्विधमुदाहृतम्॥ Kám. N. **XV. 7,8;** cf. also Hit. II. **103-105.** लोकतन्त्र—administration of a kingdom. तच्छीला॰—तत् refers to the king. प्रकृतयः— The subjects. समानभर्तृ॰— of a like disposition with the king, i. e. equally abandoned to vice. तन्त्राध्यक्षाः—Lit. the heads of different departments; the principal officers of state. स्वानि कर्मफलानि—The fruits of their own duties, i. e. the income of their offices. आयद्वागणी—The sources of revenue. विटविधेयतया—The king being in the power of, i. e. following the counsels of, his parasites or companions in sensuality. विटवैधेय॰ v.l.—on account of the follies of his parasites. उपारुढ॰— for the king did not fear adverse criticism from them. पानगो॰... कृताः— admitted to the drinking parties. अपाचरत्— had intercourse with भिन्नवृत्त—who abandoned their character, deviated from the path of virtue. मन्दत्रासाः—from fear. पांसुलजन—licentious people. भङ्गिभाषण — crooked (cleverly expressed) speeches, i. e in which there is a hidden meaning in addition to the plain grammatical meaning conveying the real intention of the speaker. भग्नचारित्र॰—breaking through the restraint (shackles) of good conduct or character. धातृगण—paramours. सामर्षा—enthusiasts, exasperated parties. अपहृतपरिभूतयः—freed from the fear reproof. अपहतपरिहृतयः v. l.—the avoiding of them being removed, i. e. there being no necessity to avoid them. प्रहताः &c—The ways of sin became trodden, i e. frequented. वधबन्धातुराः—distressed by slaughter and imprisonment, or confinement for being put to death ( वधार्थे बन्धः बधबन्धस्तेनातुरा.)

 ** P. 199** अयथाप्रणीतः—unjustly inflicted, or inflicted on the wrong party. क्रोध—disaffection, discontent. कृश—reduced to poverty. तेजस्विनो &c.—Cf. तत्तेजस्वी पुरुषः परकृतनिकृतिं कथं सहते Bhar. N. **37.** अकृत्येषु—evil acts, plots against the king. Here अ has the sense of अप्राशस्त्य. प्रासरन्—found scope to act, proved effective. परोपजापाः—intrigues of the enemy; secret overtures or instigating to rebellion. तदा च &c.—The constr. is तदा च अश्मकेन्द्रयुक्ताः तीक्ष्णरसदादयः मृगयु॰.....अग्निविसर्गैः &c. अनन्तवर्मकटकं जर्जरमकुवन्. From here are described the various sinister means employed by the secret agents of Aśmakendra to weaken the army of Anantavarman by the destruction of the eminent warriors in it अद्रिद्रोणीः— mountain-valleys. अनपसारमार्गाः—having no passage for egress, having but one entrance. शुष्क &c.—Bah qualifying द्रोणीः. द्वारतोऽग्निवि॰—setting fire to them at the entrance causing it to spread inward, which prevented all chance of escape. इष्टकूप—a desirable well, i. e. one the water of which was represented to be excellent and desirable for drinking. तट—a precipice. प्रदर—a crevice, a pit. विष्वग्विसर—moving in different directions. ०अनुयात्तया—as their servants were cut off from them. ०अपराद्धैर्नाम—under pretext that they failed (missed their aim) to hit the deer. सपणबन्धं—with a wager or stipulation (यदि भवानिदं कुर्यात् तर्हिदमहं भवते दास्यामि इति समयकरणं पणबन्धः। Manoramâ). प्रतिरोधन—way laying, attacking and making prisoners; for this sense प्रतिरोधन, of, कोदण्डपाणि निनदत्प्रतिरोधकानामापातदुष्पसहमाविरदभूनीकम्। Málav. **V. 10. **सङ्कुल—a crowd. इतरेषां हिंसोत्पा॰—By causing others to be killed by forcing them to enter crowds of people gathered on such occasions as &c., so as to get them inculpated. गूढोत्पादित॰—to whom injuries were secretly caused to be done. पराक्रमैः—by asking them to run away (पराक्रमणैः) secretly. सुहृत्वेनाभि॰—having asked paramours to approach them as their friends. साहसोपन्यासै—by mentioning these as their desperate deeds; with the reading भर्तृभयमपहृत्य 'by mentioning their daring deedŚ (to their husbands). योग॰—a beautiful woman who lures a man into danger, and destroys him; further on, it means а woman with a body fed on particular things, contact with whom leads to consumption. योग in such cases seems to mean—'what leads to the attainment of the desired object by means of the employment of spells, drugs and the like;' योगप्रधाना योगयुक्ता वा नारी; cf. words like योगचूर्ण, योगवर्तिका &c.सङ्केत—a appointed places. प्रागुप॰—first lying concealed near; the Com. needlessly takes this in the sense of लीनतां प्रापप्य. अकीर्तनीय—secret. **प्रमापण—**killing. उपप्रलोभ्य—having enticed them.

 P. 200. विघ्नव्याज॰—to be procured or encompassed under colour of their meeting with obstacles. प्रत्यपायनिवर्तन—प्रतिगतोऽपायं प्रत्यपायः a remedy against injury; तस्मान्निवर्तनैः refraining from taking steps to prevent injury, i. e. allowing the elephants to kill or wound them. व्यालहस्तिन्—a vicious elephant. अपक्रमण—directing it to; letting it off against. दायाद्यर्थी—for such things as a share of the ancestral property or patrimony. अयथावृत्तान्—those not behaving virtuously, profligates. योगाङ्गना—Some read योग्याङ्गना, and take योग्या to mean a young woman, but here अङ्गना has no propriety. रसविधान—impregnating or saturating with poison. चिकीत्सामुखेन—by way of, or under the pretence of, treating. आमयोपबृंहणैः—by fostering diseases, or making the diseases reach a more serious stage, making the cases worse. तीक्ष्णरसदा॰—such men as were entrusted with the work of striking in the vital parts (secret assasins) by administering poison and the like. Or तीक्ष्णरस may mean ‘virulent poison.' प्रवीर— choicest warrior. वानवास्य—"The king of Vanavásî, a city in the South of India, the remains of which in the Sunda district were
'discovered by Colonel Colin Mackenzie." P. Peterson.'' Vanavásî was one of the seats of the Kadamba kings in the sixth and seventh centuries and is the modern Vanavásî in North Canara. The Mahábhárata mentions वनवासिकाः, and from the context these appear to the inhabitants of Vanvâsî. The passage names all the people of the south as under—अथापरे जनपदा दक्षिणा भरतर्षभ। द्रविडाः केरलाः प्राच्या भूषिका वनवासिकाः। कर्णाटका महिषकाः &c. Bhîshma-P. IX. 58–59.” Mr. Agashe. प्रोत्साह्य—having fomented or incited against. तत्परामृष्ट &c—The borders of his kingdom being invaded by him. बलसमुत्थान—the mobilization of his forces अश्मकेन्द्रः—see p. 195. कुन्तलपतेः—Kuntala was the name of the country to the north of Ohola. Its capital was Kalyân or Kallian-Doorg. According to Mr. Vincent Smith, Kuntala was the country between the Bhîmâ and the Vedavatî, bounded on the West by the Ghats including Śimoga, Chitaldroog, Bellary, Dharwar, and Bijapur, and the adjacent parts. Early History, p. 148.आत्मनाटकीया—a dancing girl in his service. परामृशति—violates.

 P. 201. मुरलेशं—The lord of the Kerala country. Muralá is the principal river of Kerala (mentioned by Kálidása, Ragh. IV. 55). Hence the people of Kerala were sometimes called Muralas. Kerala was the strip of land between the Western Ghats and the sea, north of the Kâverî It corresponds to the modern Cinara, and probably included Malabar also. ऋषीक—and नासिक्य, were countries of the South which, together with the others mentioned, form the whole of Southern India. The reading ऋषिक is adopted from Mr. Agashe's ed. in preference to the old reading ऋचीक as it is found in the Rámáyaṇs (विदर्भानृपिकांश्चैव रम्यान्माहिषिकनपि IV. 41) and in the Mahábhârata Bhîshma-P. IX. 5. 64, and also in the Bṛihatsaṃhitâ XIV. 15 ( तापमाश्रमा ऋषिक ). Násikya is mentioned among the southern countries in the Bṛih. Saṃ. in the verse कर्णाटमहाटविचित्रकूटनासिक्यकोल्लगिरिचोल्लाः। &c. XIV. 13. According to some Nâsikya is the present Nâsik on the Godâvarî. उपजपाव—make secret overtures to win over to our side; instigate to rebellion. अग्रतो व्यतिषक्तं—engaged or encountered in front. काञ्चन॰—shawls of a saffron hue with lace-borders. प्राभृतीकृत्य—offering as presents, presenting. आमिषत्वमगमत्—fell a victim to them; i. e. was overpowered by them and lost his kingdom. अवशीर्ण—lit. routed; hence, got as spoil. ०आत्माधिष्ठीतं—keeping it in his custody. यथाबलं—according to your respective powers.सर्वानुवर्ती— pleasing all, अग्रसत्—appropriated to himself.

 मौलैः—hereditary ministers. दाहज्वर—a burning fever brought on by grief. माहिष्मतीं—It was the capital of the Haihayas or Kalachuris (the great Kàrtavîrya and his son Arjuna), whose kingdom lay to the North of the Narmadá. It was situated between the Vindhya and the Ṛiksha mountains, about Beraghar, below Jabalpur. See Ragh. VI. 43. ०द्वैमातुराय भ्रावे—her husband's brother by another mother. अन्यथाऽभ्यमन्यत—thought otherwise of her; i. e. thought that she would yield to his desires if he made the proposal अवधाय—making sure that it would be a place of safety.

 P. 202 क्षेमप्रवृत—gone in safety. घोष—a station or residence of cowherds, आश्वासयितुं—to invigorate or refresh सम्पात—a sudden attack. किमीया जात्या—what is her family? सस्वजे—Another instance of the author's inadvertence (Perf. in the 1st pers.). सिन्धुदत्तापुत्राणां— Sindhudattâ seems to be the name of SuŚruta's mother, his father being Pushpodbhava.
नयावलिप्त—proud of his state-craft. अविष्यम्—I shot the arrows at. एकः &c.—One of the deer fell with the arrow piercing him to its feathery part, the other with te arrow piercing him through, so as to leave its feathers behind. सपत्रनिष्पत्रादतिव्यथने Páṇ. **V. 4. 61;** डाच् (aff. आ) स्यात् सपत्राकरोति मृगम्। सपुङ्खशरप्रवेशनेन सपत्रं करोतीत्यर्थः। निष्पत्राकरोति सपुङ्खस्य शरस्यापरपार्श्वेन निर्गमनात् निष्पत्रं करोतीत्यर्थः। Sid.-Kau. मृगयु—मृगान् वधार्थं यातीति; a huntsman. **अपलोमत्वचः—**which was skinned and cleaned. क्लोम—the lungs, or entrails. निष्कुलाकृत्य—having dissected or separated the body into different parts. निर्गतं कुलमन्तरवयवानां समूहो यस्मादिति बहुव्रीहेर्डाच्। निष्कुलाकरोति दाडिमम्। Sid-Kau. on Pa'ṇ. **V. 4. 62** (sее com.). शूलाकृत्य—spitting it with a rod and roasting it. आ is added to निष्कुल and शूल in the senses given (see com). The author seems to have used these words here purposely, to display his knowledge of Sanskrit grammar.

 **P. 203**. अत्यतार्षम्—अतार्षम् seems to be a mistaken form for अतारिषं which is the proper form. अतक्षिषं v. 1. must be taken in the sense of 'satisfied.' दृति—a leather-bag. सम्यक्प्रतिपत्त्या— by (treating or acting towards) her well. प्रत्याकृष्य—having attracted him back. आक्रोशन—a report. अभिपत्स्यति—The Paras. is irregular; or read ०पत्स्यते, 'will approach her (with carnal desire ); cf. रावणावरजा तत्र राघवं मदनातुरा। अभिपेदे निदाघार्ता ब्यालीव मलयद्रुमम्॥ **Ragh. XII. 32**. सन्नीय—having mixed. पतिव्रता इति—supply उक्त्वा after this (इति उक्त्वा हन्तव्यः) कापालिक—a mendicant of a particular Śaiva sect, moving with a skull in his hand for collecting alms, &c.

 **P. 204.** **रेवा—**or ‘the roaring one,' was another name for the Narmadá. परीक्ष्य वैजन्यं जनेषु निर्गतेषु—when people will have left it after assuring themselves that there was no one in. प्रतिष्ठापयिष्यति—will re-establish him in his sovereignty. उपपत्स्यते—all this will come to pass. उपधियुक्तं—based on, or proceeding from, fraud. सानाथ्यशंसी &c.—The vision declared that she (Mañjuvádinî) had a protector (was no longer destitute), referring to her espousal by Viśruta.

 ** P. 205. मद्दर्शनराग॰**—who was agitated on account of the passion she felt for me at my sight. मञ्जुवादिनी॰—whose cour age was drunk up by the loveful glances of Mañjuvádinî. आकार्य—having made a sign to him to follow us प्रथितः—much talked of. कुशीलव—musicians, bards.
मात्राः—chattels, property; here, his equipments. ०वेषलीलः—having put on the gay dress of.अनुरञ्जितातपे समये—at a time when the sun-light had turned red, i e. when it was about sunset. समाजज्ञानोप॰—the reading समाजोपयोगीनि is better, as ज्ञान has no particular propriety here. नानारुतानि—imitations of different voices. ०रुदितानि v. l. is bad, as रुदित is not applied to the notes of birds. हस्तचङ्क्रमण—moving on hands with the legs raised up. ऊर्ध्वपाद &c.—movements with different postures of the body, for which see com करणानि—gymnastic feats.

 ** P. 206.** **उपहितवर्ष्मा—** with these fixed about my body. **उत्क्रोशपात—** jumping like an osprey. **प्रत्युरसं—**अ is added generally to उरस् at the end of an Avyayîbháva. वसन्तभानुः—The king of Aśmaka. His object was to inculpate Vasantabhánu as the instigator of the murder. उत्कर्तुं—अरुकर्तुं v. l. is the reading of both the Bhúshaṇá and the Laghudîpiká. It means 'to wound,' being der. from अरस्. The final स् of this, as also of चेतस् and चक्षुस्, is dropped before the aff. च्वि; see com. चारभटः—a valorous man, a warrior; or, it may mean here one of the bird-warriors; see com. आक्रम्य— having jumped upon विचेतीकुर्वन्—stunning him, making him senseless. आकुलं—frightened, confused. एष पन्थाः—this line of my foot-marks. ०अस्पष्ट &c.—my foot-prints being effaced from the sand levelled by. पुनरवाचा—then turning to the west. In this case वप्र will mean the mound that supports a rampart. उच्चितेष्टक &c.—the bricks being unevenly piled up. खात—a ditch. प्रतिमुक्त—having put on. मत्कर्मतुमुल—crowded with people full of talk about my deed प्रतिमाधिष्ठान—the place or pedestal of the image. भग्नपार्श्व॰—the outer opening of which was concealed with a large stone-slab the sides of which were loosened.

 **P. 207**. वर्षधर—a eunuch. देवी तु—i.e. the queen Vasuṃdhará. अश्मकेन्द्रो॰— sent him word that the unhappy event had been cotrived by the king of AŚmaka. ०कुक्षिवैजन्य—carefully ascertained that the interior was quite unoccupied. पटियांसं—very loud. अणुतर—very minute. लोहपादपीठ—the iron pedestal- अंसलपुरुष॰—not to be shaken even by a strong man after effort. The possessive aff. ल after अंस signifies strength; see com. यथापूर्वमर्पयित्वा—after having replaced Durgá. प्रत्यय-realization, confidence. This and the following are adverbs, to be construed with प्रणिपतन्तीः. रूढविस्मयं—overpowered by astonishment. आपन्नः—in distress, plunged into misery. अमन्दमातृपक्षः—one whose mother's side is strong; i. e. having a protecting mother The reading मन्द॰ makes no sense. ०**घट्टनात्मानं—** whose son (energy, spirit) is able to remove (break off) the pot in the shape of As maka, cruel by his intrigue well brought to light &c. निर्वेश—compensation, recompense. आर्यया—by the god dess. वाचामगोचरा—not to be expressed by words, beyond the power of expression

 P. 208. अलब्धरन्भ्रः—who could find no weak point, who could find no trace of our contrivance or secret plot. नष्टमुष्टि &c.—By telling what a man has lost, or what, he has in his clenched hand (or whether the things in his clenched,hand are of even or odd number), or what one is, thinking of and such other things—proofs which a man having, any pretence to divine knowledge must give. समर्थयमानः—upholding; be-lieving in. आर्यापुत्रः—son of the venerable goddess. प्रभावहेतुः—due to the divine power of the goddess; it may also be a Gen. Tatp. भ्रदाकृतं—shaved (for an auspicious purpose). मन्त्र—the power arising from good counsel, प्रभाव—the power arising from the majesty or pre-eminent position of the king. उत्साह—the personal energy of the king. परस्परा॰—mutually assisted by their right employment. क्रमन्ते—have effect, have power to accomplish. The next sentence explains their respective spheres of action. विनिश्चयः &c.—the determination of objects or political measures. निर्वहणं—the realization of the object. पञ्चाङ्ग—the five elements of statesmanship are:—allies, the means to the end, a proper division of time or space, adopting counter-measures (to meet an emergency), and attainment of the desired object. पञ्च अङ्गानि यस्यासौ पञ्चाङ्गो मन्त्रः मूलं यस्य। See Kâm. Nîtis. XII. 26 quoted in the .com. Policy or state-craft is here compared to a tree composed of the different parts mentioned, here. द्विरूपप्रभावः—The supremacy of the king's position is due to two things, vis. greatness of wealth and abundance of men. उत्साह—has four divisions, viz. the well-known four Upayas (साम &c.); or exertion of mind, speech, body and action. द्विसप्ततिप्रकृति॰— द्विसप्ततिः प्रकृतयो यथा - (1) मध्यमः (2) विजिगीषुः (3) उदासीनः शत्रुः; एता मूलप्रकृतयः। (1) मित्रं (2) अरिमित्रं (3) मित्रमित्रम् (4) अरिमित्रमित्रम्, एता विजिगीषोः पुरोवर्तिन्यः शाखाप्रकृतयः। अथ चतस्रः शाखाप्रकृतयः पृष्ठगाः यथा - (1) पार्ष्णिग्राहः (2) आक्रन्दः (३) पार्ष्णिग्राहासारः (4) आक्रन्दासारः। एवमष्टौ शाखामकृतयः। पञ्चद्रव्यप्रकृतयो यथा— (1) अमात्यः (2) राष्ट्रं (3) दुर्गं (4) कोषः (5) दण्डः। एताः पूर्वोक्तद्वादशप्रकृतीनामेकैकस्याः भवन्ति। अत एताः (12 x 5) षष्टिः सम्पद्यन्ते। ताश्च द्वादशभिर्मूलप्रकृतिभिर्मिलिता द्विसप्ततिर्भवन्ति। (T. Kaviratna). Cf. also मौला द्वादश चैवैता अमात्याद्यास्तथा च याः। सप्ततिर्द्व्यधिकाद्येषां सर्व प्रकृतिमण्डलम्॥ Kám N. XII. 25. शक्तिसिध्दी॰—power and success are the flowers and fruits respectively. नेतुः—The man at the head of political affairs; the king or the prime minister. For a similar fig. comp. Mudr. V. 1; and सखे त्वन्निति पादपस्य पुष्पमुध्दिन्नम्.। Málav. I.; and for a similar description of. अष्टशाखं चतुर्मूल षष्टिपत्रं द्वये स्थितम्। षट्पुष्पं त्रिफलं वृक्षं यो जानाति स नीतिवित्। Kám. N. VIII. 42. अनेकधिकार॰—on account of its dependence on various parts or elements. दुरपजीव्यः— difficult to be turned to advantage, hard to be made to yield fruit. कोसला॰—being a native of Kosala. अवमत्य—because he (Mitravarmâ ) despised his counsel. from

 P. 209. रहस्यशिक्षयम्—The instruction given here is in close conformity with Châṇakya's precepts in Kauți. Prak. VI. under the heading उपधाभिः शौचाशौचज्ञानममात्यानाम्. मायापुरुषः—a deceitful person, a rogue. भुजङ्ग—a snake, a gallant; both the senses are here intended. प्राभृत —presents, bribes. अतिविस्त्रम्भ॰—When he was in a pleased mood; or, when I got an opportunity owing to the confidence engendered or gained. त्वदुपदिष्टेन नयेन—according to your direction or instruction. शुध्दिदर्शनं—giving evidence of the nobility or purity of. अनुग्रहार्द्रं—tender with, disposed to favour. अभ्यमित्रिण—valliant enough to overpower his enemies. The affixes छ, यत् or ख ( ईन as here ) are added to अमित्र with अभि in the sense of 'boldly facing an enemy,' by Pàṇ. V. 2. 17; see com.; so अभ्यमित्रीय and अभ्यमित्र्य also. सन्निपातिनः—collected together. चिरबिल्वद्रुम—poisonous tree; i. e. a destroyer of. प्रह्न—submissive. नीतिज्ञम्मन्य—आत्मानं नीतिज्ञं मन्यतेऽसौ. उपधाभिर्विशोध्य—having tested his sincerity by various special trials. उपधीयते शुद्धिज्ञानमत्र इत्युपधा; from धा + अ (अङ्) by आतश्चापसर्गे Pân. III. 3. 106. It means a trial by making a man swerve from Dharma, Káma, and Artha. It is said to be of four kinds, viz. (1) loyalty, (2) disinterestedness, (3) con- tinence, and (4) courage. Brâhmaṇas and ministers should be so tested. Cf. धर्मोपधाभिर्विप्रांश्च सर्वाभिः सचिवान्पुनः। (शोधयेत्) Kálikâ.-P. It is different from उपधि and उपाधि, also derived from धा. मतिसहाय—an accomplice in counsel or state-craft. शौच—
uprightness, honesty विविधव्यञ्जनान्—moving about under various disguises. गूढपुरुष—a spy, a secret agent. लुब्धसमृद्ध—covetous misers. अत्युत्सिक्त—very proud. अविधेयप्राय—mostly turbulent अभिख्यापयन्—proclaiming as commendable.

 P.210. उद्भावयन्—creating a taste or liking for. उपधि—a wily trick, a stratagem. अभिसमाहरमर्थान्—I collected the revenue. अर्थमूल—proceeding from wealth. दण्ड—stands for दण्डनीति; 'the commencement of all undertakings based on policy.' तत्र दौर्बल्यात्—weakness in that, i. c. अर्थ (having a good treasury ). योग—stratagems, political measures (to gain one's ends).

 आज्ञया—when I give the command. राज्यद्वितय॰—see com. नीत्याविष्ट—guided by policy, always acting in accordance with the rules of policy. च—in addition to that.

 P. 211. चलचित्त—vacillating, uncertain. मौलाः प्रकृतयः—hereditary subjects, i. e. the people of the Vidarbha country. आवर्जन—bestowal, conferring. उपजप्ताः—overtures were thus made to them. वाच्यमेव—we are bound to express it. विश्रुत—renowned. जन्यवृतिः—engaged in actual warfare; i. e. does not take arms against. स वीतभयः &c.—The sense is somewhat obscure. He who comes over to his side stands out of the reach of danger and obtaining great fortune &c. सर्वेभ्यो वार्तम्—the message has been communicated to you all. भिन्नमना इव &c.—appear to have their hearts estranged from me (ie. their affection alienated from me ). क्षमामवलम्ब्य—remaining passive or apathetic. उत्पन्नोपजापं—in whom seeds of dissension have been sown. मदवबोधकं—my purpose resolution to fight.

 P. 212. क्षणं—only for a moment; i, e. cannot long hold out. अन्यायेन—goes with परराज्यक्रमण. परराज्य॰—an unjust or unprovoked expedition is condemned by Hindu writers on politics. पुरोऽभवत्—faced the army. आलेख्यलिखितेव—like painted figures. सङ्गर—a duel. शिक्षाविशेष— superior skill. अतः परं—after this, i. e. the fall of the king. स्वस्ववृति—their respective inoomes. तद्वशवर्तिनः समभवन्—Lit. delivered themselves into his power; acknowledged him as their sovereign. राजसूनुसात्—entirely into his possession, अनेहस्—time.

 P. 213. ॰लक्षण—marked by राज्यधूः—This ought to be राज्यधुरा; see Pân. V. 4. 74. सचिवरत्न— excellent minister. आश्रट—importunity. उत्कलाधि॰—Utkala, the modern Orissa, lay to the South of Támralipta and extended to the river Kapiśá यावत्प्रयाणोपक्रमं &c.—As soon as I proposed to go. आकारित—called. पाटलिपुरे यौवराज्यं &c.—The reader will remember that Somadatta was asked by Râjavâhana to convey his wife to his capital (i.e. Páṭalî, see p. 30, where we should have read पाटलीं नाम्रा तस्य &c.), the town of Vîraketu, where he was reiging as Yuvarája. See p. 42. सप्रमोदसंवलिताः—सप्रमोदाश्च ताः संवलिताश्च mixed, and full of joy. उत्कील्य—having opened. आशास्य—after expressing his blessings.

 P. 214. स्कन्धावार—army; lit. what consists of divisions. उदन्वत्—the sea. निश्चयं—our resolution to die. विज्ञान—superhuman knowledge. भाग्योदयात—their fortune smiling upon them. षोडशाब्दान्ते—This, if the correct reading, must be taken to mean 'at the close of their sixteenth year.' In this case we shall have to suppose that the princes started on their tour of conquest at the beginning of their sixteenth year. It is clear, however, from some statements in the preceding narratives, that the princes started on their mission after their attainment of youth, i. e. at the close of the 16th year. So it will be better to read, अब्दान्ते , as we cannot reasonably suppose the Kumârás to have been for sixteen years in search of Rájaváhana, अवधि—The limit of time fixed by the ascetic, víz. 16 years. तत्प्रवृत्ति—the doings of the princes. आकारणाय—to call you.

 P. 215. पानीयमपि &c.—Cf. the similar message of Tárápîḍa to Chandrâpîḍa at Kàd. p. 328. समर्थतर—very able, मानसारः—There seems to be some confusion here. Not Mánasára, but Darpasára was the reigning monarch then. But, as he was away, Mánasára might be supposed to have taken up the reins of the government of Ujjayinî after the assasination at Ohampá of Chaṇḍavarman, Darpasára's regent. निहतश्च—This seems to be strange; for it was Mânasára who had prevented Chaṇḍavarmā from putting Rája. to immediate death; so this ought to be omitted. प्रत्यागमनान्तं—upto their return from their starting on that expedition अतिदुर्घट—extremely marvellous.

 P. 216. अवाङ्मनस॰—notice the comp. ending; a final अ is added to वाङ्मनस by Pâṇ. V. 4. 77. वानप्रस्थाश्रमं—The third stage of life in which a Dvija ought to stay in a forest living as a hermit; see Ya'jñavalkya quoted in the com.; of. also Manu. VI 3, आत्मसाधन— final emancipation by contemplation यथोदितं— According to the success or rise (उद्+इत) attained by each Or— according to your direction (उदित p p p. of वद्) कायक्लेशं विना— without having to suffer bodily hardships or mortification. परिजनः—his vassals, the other Kumâras,

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

** Appendices.**

No. I.

BOMBAY UNIVERSITY EXAMINATION

QUESTIONS.

(N. B.—Questions repeated are omitted).

1871

 I. Write a short account, in Sanskrit, of the manner in which Rájaváhana's nine companions came under the care of his father, as related in the Pûrvapîṭhiká. II. (a) Translate into idiomatic English:— इतः किल जनाद्भगवतः....श्रेयसेऽप्यनल्पाय कल्पते। pp. 69-70. (b) Who speaks these words, under what circumstances, and with what intention? (c) With what intention has the poet put in this whole scene, and especially this speech? III. (a) Translate into English:— अन्यदा तु वियति व्यवदायमानचन्द्रिके...सरोषरभसमपासरत्। P. 60. (b) Who speaks these words and who is the actor in the scene described? (c) Explain grammatically व्यवदायमान, give the etymology of वियत् , and state the rules regarding the formation of the feminines अमृतस्यन्दिनी and सुप्तगात्री. Parse the word पाण्डुलोहश्रृङ्खलात्मना.

                                                                  
1872

 I. Correct any mistakes that may occur in the text or the translation, here given of the following passages:— (a) वेणुयष्टिमादाय तच्छायितया परिखायां स्थापितया च प्राकारमत्यलङ्घयम्। ( b) तया विना न भोक्ष्य इति रुदन्त्येव स्थास्यति। (c) वार्द्धकोचितमन्त्यमाश्रमं सङ्क्रमेयं यदि देवः साधु मन्यते। (d) न च शक्ष्यामि राजसूनुरित्यमुष्मिन्न्यायमाचरितुम्।— 'I shall not be able to follow a proper conduct or loyalty to him (although he be) a friend.' (e) अतिष्ठच्च सा सद्य एव मे हृदये न मयाऽन्येन चान्तराले दृष्टा— 'although seen by me, a difforent person, and at a distance, she took her place immediately in my heart.’ (One MS. reads न मयाऽन्येन वाऽन्तराले हटा). (f) प्रावर्तत सम्प्रहारः पराजयिषत यवनाः। (g) तमप्यार्द्राशयः स्कन्धेनोदूह्य गहनोद्देशे चिरमवसत्। (h) पुष्टं च तं सा धूमिनी रिरंसयोपातिष्ठत्। (i) अथ परप्रत्ययाहृतेषु दारेषु यादृच्छिकीं सम्पत्तिमनभिसमीक्ष्य; 'not approving of accidental coming together in wives taken upon faith in others. ' (j) दत्त्वा पटबन्धनं सा भयाऽपदेशादपरं चापनीय नूपुरं.......दिनानि निन्ये। (k) तेन जामाता कृतोऽस्मि दामलिप्तेश्वरेण तत्पुत्रो मदनुजीविजातः। (l) सन्नतानङ्ग्या सङ्गत्यारंसिषि। (m) निजनिकेतनं न्यशिश्रयम्। (n) चन्द्रशेखरनरकशासनत्रिदशेशानाम्। ‘of Śiva, Yama, Brahma, &c.' (o) चाणक्योपदिष्टानाहरणोपायान्सहस्रधात्मबुद्धयैते विकल्पयितारः। (p) यावता च नयेन विना लोकयात्रा स लोकत एव सिद्धो न तत्र शास्त्रेणार्थः— 'As long as the course of the world (proceeds) without policy, the object (of government) seems to be accomplished, through the world (by ordinary means; or of its own nature or spontaneously), not by the Śástras.' Two MSS. read: नयेन विना न याति लोकयात्रा। (q) तदा च मृगयुवेषता (other ed. मृगयुवेष)—मृगबाहुल्यवर्णने—नाद्रिद्रोणीः प्रवेश्य। (r) प्रचण्डवर्मा मञ्जुवादिनीं विलिप्सुरभ्येति। (Only the corrections of the words which are actually wrong are required. The whole passages need not be repeated, nor need reasons be assigned for the corrections). II. Translate into English:—द्यूतेऽपि द्रव्यराशेस्तृणवत्यागा......मभ्युपायरचनया बुद्धिवाचोः पाटवम्। pp. 196-197. III. Give the etymology of अपत्य, अमात्य, कन्या, क्षण, तुरङ्ग, तूष्णीम्, दुहितु, नैर्ऋत, भुज, वासर.

1876

 1. Translate into English:— either (a) या वसन्तसहायेन समुत्सुकतया रतेः...निर्मितेव रराज। pp. 44-45, or (b) उदारकस्तु तदादाय सलज्जं च......को वाऽहं यथा त्वमाज्ञापयसीति। pp. 79-80. II. Correct the mistakes against grammar in the following passages:— (only the corrections should be given). (a) ततः स रत्नाकरमेखलामनन्यशासनां शासन्ननपत्यतया नारायणमर्चयामास। (b) कदाचिदेकेन तापसेन सुकुमारं कुमारं राज्ञे समर्प्यावोचि। (c) बालकं पल्लवकवलमिवाददति गजपतौ कण्ठीरवो महाग्रहेण न्यपतत्। (d) बालकं निजोत्सङ्गतले निधाय स्थविरामेकां रुदन्तीं विलोक्यावोचिषम्। (e) नदीवेगेनोह्यमाना केनचित्तरूलग्नेन कालभोगिनाऽहमदंशि। (f) कमप्याक्रीड मासाद्य विशश्रमिषुरान्दोलिकारूढं पुरुषमपश्यत्। (g) कार्पण्यविवर्णवदनो महदाशापूर्णमानसोऽवोचदग्रजन्मा। (h) कुमारः सत्वरमानेतव्यो मया ने चेदेनां स्मरणीयां गतिं नयिष्यति मीनकेतनः। (i) उन्नततरुफलमलभ्य त्वदुरःस्थलालिङ्गनसौख्यं स्मरान्धतया लिप्सुः। (j) शयेयं भावितविषवेगविक्रियस्त्वयाऽप्यमी वाच्याः। III. (a) Judging from the whole style of the DaŚakumáracharita, do you think that the mistakes enumerated under question II. should be corrected? (b) Have you found in the the DaŚakum. any words derived from the Greek or Latin? (c) Explain the meaning of the following words or phrases:— अग्रहार, काकली, घुणाक्षरन्यायेन, नैष्ठिक, श्वोवसीय, क्षपणक, निर्ग्रन्थिक, ऐकागारिक.

1877

  I. Translate:— (a) लीलाशिथिलं च भूमौ मुक्तवती। मन्दोत्थितं च &c….मध्यविलम्बितलये द्रुतलये च.........चूर्णपदमदर्शयत्। p. 151. (b) ततस्तस्य शाल्योदनस्य...... दध्ना च त्रिजातकावचूर्णितेन...दयाचत च पानीयम्। pp. 162, 163. II. (a) Explain the following words and expressions:— पूर्वेषुकामचर, उपधाय वामभुजम्, लक्षसुप्तः, अलसतान्त तारकेण युगिलेन चक्षुषोः, परिवादिनीद्वितीयः, मृजावन्तः निर्ग्रन्थिका, घनदर्शितरागविभ्रमा विद्युल्लतामिव विडम्बयन्ती, उदञ्चनम्, भस्माने हुतम्. (b) Explain the grammar of the following, and state if any of the forms are incorrect, giving the corresponding correct ones:— द्वैमातुरः, उपास्थायिषि मधुवर्षमवर्षत् पर्यश्रुः, अभ्यधत्त,अभ्यधीये, अदत्त.

1897

 1. State the meaning in which the following words are used in the DaŚakumáracharita:— चारक, गम्य, मातृका, पटभास, नीवी, कारणा, औशीर, सुषीम. II. Translate into English:— एष हि गणिकामातुरधिकारो......०नर्थसंशयान्विचार्य भूयोभूयः संयोजनमिति। pp. 66-68. III. Write a brief account of the state of society mirrored in the DaŚakumáracharita as regards (1) morality, and (2) education.

1898

 1. Translate into English:— एवं गते मन्त्रिणि राजनि य दोषगुणा स्वेच्छया विपरिवर्तयतां मतं गुरूपदेशमिवादरेणानुवर्तमाने तच्छीलानुसारिण्यः प्रकृतयो विशृङ्खलमसेवन्त व्यसनानि। सर्वश्च स्वसमानदोषतया न कस्यचिच्छिद्रान्वेषणायायतिष्ट। समानभर्तृप्रकृतयस्तन्त्राध्यक्षाः स्वानि स्वानि कर्मफलान्यभक्षयन्। ततः क्रमादायद्वाराणि विशीर्णानि। व्ययमुखानि विटविधेयतया विभोरहरहर्व्यवर्धन्त। सर्वेऽपि स्वं स्वमाचारमत्यचारिषुः। अहन्यन्त दुर्बला बलिभिः। अपहृतपरिहृतयः प्रहताः पातकपथाः। दण्डश्चायथाप्रणीतो भयक्रोधावजनयत्। विमानिताश्व मानेनादह्यन्त। तेषु तेषु चाकृत्येषु प्रासरन्परोपजापाः। तदा च विषमुखीभिः क्षुरिकाभिः कण्टकोद्धरणैर्मृगदेहापराद्धैर्नामेषुमोक्षर्गुढोत्पादितव्यलीकेभ्योऽप्रियाणि प्रकाशं लब्ध्वा साक्षिषु तद्विख्याप्याकीर्तिगुप्तिहेतुभिः पराक्रमैरन्यैश्चाभ्युपायैः प्रक्षपितप्रवीरमवन्तिवर्मकटकं परनियुक्ता जर्जरमकुर्वन्। (cf. pp. 196-200). II. Sketch briefly the account of Pramati. III. Give the meanings of करण, उपहस्तिका, अमित्र, मद्गु, नरेन्द्र, कृतान्त and भुजिष्या.

1920 (Inter. Arts)

 I. Translate and explain with reference to the context:— (a) सा तन्नयनाभिमुखैः किञ्चिदाकुञ्चितैः &c...... शरव्यायमाणमानसो बभूव। p. 45; (b) सखि चन्द्रमस वडवानलात् &c... कमलं विहन्यते। pp. 48-49; (c) सखि छायावत् &c.... आलबालमभूत्। p. 50; (d) दृष्टमिदानीमौदार्यस्य....... प्रज्ञाधिक्षेपः। p. 80. II. Point out the resemblances between the Mṛichchhakaṭika and the Da'sakumáracharita. Or, compare the Púrvapîthiká and the first and the second Uchchhvāsas of the DaŚak. in respect of style. III (a) To what parts of your prose text are the terms Pu'rvapîthika' and DaŚakumáracharita applied, and are they properly applied? (b) What do you know of the following from your text— (1) Madanamahotsava, (2) Gambling houses; (3) The night-guards of a town?
